|:------:|:------------|:------|:----------------------:|:------------------------:|
| **S1** | First Heart Sound | Closure of Mitral (M1) and Tricuspid (T1) valves | **30 – 150 Hz** | Beginning of systole |
| **S2** | Second Heart Sound | Closure of Aortic (A2) and Pulmonary (P2) valves | **50 – 200 Hz** | End of systole / start of diastole |
| **S3** | Third Heart Sound (Ventricular Gallop) | Rapid ventricular filling | **20 – 70 Hz** | Early diastole |
| **S4** | Fourth Heart Sound (Atrial Gallop) | Atrial contraction pushing blood into stiff ventricle | **20 – 30 Hz** | Late diastole (pre-systole) |
| **Murmurs** | Turbulent blood flow (stenosis / regurgitation) | Pathological | **20 – 1000 Hz** | Systolic / Diastolic / Continuous |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"Listening to a Drum Through a Microphone Instead of Your Ear"**
>
> Imagine you are sitting beside a friend who is tapping a drum. If you put your ear close to the drum, you can hear the sound, but you cannot easily *measure* it, *time* it, or *compare* it with another tap. Now, suppose you place a sensitive microphone on the drum's surface, connect it to an amplifier and a paper-tape recorder. Suddenly, every tap becomes a tall line, every soft tap becomes a short line, and you can *measure*, *store*, and *analyze* the pattern.
>
> The heart works exactly like that drum. A normal human ear (or a stethoscope) hears the sounds, but a **Phonocardiograph "hears," "measures," and "draws"** them on paper so that a cardiologist can later study the timing, amplitude, and frequency of every sound — including the very faint ones that the human ear cannot detect.

### Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Reference Sound Pressure Level (SPL):** $20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ (the threshold of human hearing at **1 kHz**)
> - **Standard Stethoscope Bell Diameter:** **25 – 35 mm** (low-frequency pickup)
> - **Standard Diaphragm Diameter:** **40 – 50 mm** (high-frequency pickup)
> - **Clinical Bandwidth of Interest for PCG:** **20 Hz – 1000 Hz** (sometimes extended to 2 kHz for pediatric murmurs)
> - **Cardiac Auscultation Areas on the Chest:** Aortic, Pulmonary, Tricuspid, and Mitral (apex)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Six Logical Stages

A Phonocardiograph is not a single device; it is a **signal-conditioning chain** that processes an acoustic pressure wave. The transformation proceeds in six disciplined stages:

1. **Acoustic Pickup (Coupling)** — The chest piece (bell, diaphragm, or microphone housing) is coupled to the skin with an air-tight seal so that body-surface vibrations are transmitted efficiently to the transducer.
2. **Transduction** — Acoustic pressure is converted into a proportional electrical voltage. Piezoelectric, electromagnetic, or condenser transducers are used. The transfer function is essentially:
   $$V_{\text{out}}(t) = K \cdot P(t)$$
   where $K$ is the transducer's sensitivity (V/Pa) and $P(t)$ is the instantaneous acoustic pressure.

3. **Preamplification** — The raw transducer output is in the **microvolt to millivolt** range. A low-noise preamplifier (often instrumentation-grade with CMRR > **80 dB**) raises the signal to a few hundred millivolts.

4. **Frequency-Selective Filtering (Band Shaping)** — A multi-stage band-pass filter network isolates the clinically relevant band. The standard filter set in a clinical PCG offers:
   - **L (Low) Filter** : 20 – 200 Hz
   - **M1 (Medium-1) Filter** : 50 – 500 Hz
   - **M2 (Medium-2) Filter** : 100 – 800 Hz
   - **H (High) Filter** : 200 – 1000 Hz
5. **Power Amplification** — A main amplifier drives the display/recording transducer (galvanometric pen, oscilloscope, or computer ADC).
6. **Display / Recording** — Either an analog **thermal/paper chart recorder**, a **cathode-ray oscilloscope (CRO)**, or — in modern digital PCG — a **PC sound card / DSP processor** for FFT analysis.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **high-yield formulas** routinely tested in KTU Module-3 questions. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use |
|:-:|:--------|:-------------------|:----------------|
| 1 | $V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot P(t)$ | $K$ = sensitivity (V/Pa); $P$ = acoustic pressure | Transducer output |
| 2 | $f_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $L$ = inductance; $C$ = capacitance | Tuned filter center frequency |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{f_{\text{res}}}{\Delta f}$ | $Q$ = quality factor; $\Delta f$ = −3 dB bandwidth | Filter sharpness |
| 4 | $G_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | $G$ = gain; $V$ = voltage | Amplifier gain (dB) |
| 5 | $f_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $R$ = resistance; $C$ = capacitance | First-order RC filter cutoff |
| 6 | $S_{\text{noise}} = \sqrt{4 k_{\text{B}} T R \Delta f}$ | $k_{\text{B}}$ = Boltzmann const.; $T$ = abs. temp.; $R$ = source R | Johnson thermal noise |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | $P$ = power | Signal-to-noise ratio |
| 8 | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | $V_n$ = harmonic amplitude | Distortion measure |
| 9 | $Z_{\text{mic}} = j \omega L$ (ideal) | Inductive mic impedance | Dynamic mic model |
| 10 | $q = d \cdot F$ (piezo) | $d$ = charge coeff.; $F$ = force | Piezo charge output |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> A Phonocardiograph system is fundamentally a **low-frequency, low-amplitude acoustic spectrometer**. The same signal-conditioning chain appears (with different center frequencies) in:
> - **Electronic stethoscopes** (telescoping cardiac telemedicine)
> - **Fetal heart-rate monitors** (detecting fetal heart tones)
> - **Seismocardiography (SCG)** — chest-wall vibration mapping
> - **Vibroarthrography** — knee-joint sound analysis
> - **Industrial acoustic-emission testing** for crack detection

Thus, mastering PCG instrumentation is a **gateway skill** to all body-vibration diagnostic systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of a First-Order High-Pass Filter (Used to Reject Low-Frequency Body Drift)

A standard RC high-pass stage is the workhorse of every clinical PCG. We derive its transfer function rigorously, because KTU frequently asks for the **−3 dB cutoff frequency** and the **filter gain expression**.

**Step 1 — Apply the Voltage Divider in the Frequency Domain**

The output is taken across the series resistor $R$ when the input $V_{\text{in}}(s)$ is applied to a series capacitor $C$ followed by $R$ to ground.

$$
\frac{V_{\text{out}}(s)}{V_{\text{in}}(s)} \;=\; \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{sC}}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $sC$**

$$
H(s) \;=\; \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ for Steady-State Sinusoidal Excitation**

$$
H(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Magnitude**

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert \;=\; \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the Cutoff Frequency where $\vert H\vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$**

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{\omega_c RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}}
$$

Squaring and rearranging yields:

$$
\omega_c RC = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

This is the classic expression used in KTU numerical problems.

> **Conversion Logic Step-by-Step:**
> - We replaced time-domain variables with their Laplace equivalents ($R \to R$, $C \to 1/sC$).
> - The s-domain equation was normalized by multiplying by $sC$ to remove the fractional form.
> - Substituting $s = j\omega$ converts Laplace to Fourier, giving the sinusoidal steady-state response.
> - Magnitude of the complex ratio yields a real, frequency-dependent gain.
> - The −3 dB condition is the engineering definition of "cutoff" — the point where output power is halved.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quality Factor $Q$ of a Second-Order Band-Pass Filter (Used in the "Murmur Band")

The band-pass filter used in the **M2 (100–800 Hz)** setting is realized with a series RLC circuit. The Q-factor derivation is a KTU favorite.

**Step 1 — Transfer Function of a Series RLC Band-Pass**

$$
H(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{R}{R + j\omega L + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $j\omega C$**

$$
H(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 LC + j\omega RC}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Resonant (Center) Frequency**

The denominator's real part becomes zero when:

$$
\omega_0^2 LC = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}
$$

**Step 4 — Bandwidth in Terms of R, L, C**

The −3 dB points occur where the imaginary part equals the real part. The classical result is:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{R}{2\pi L}
$$

**Step 5 — Quality Factor Definition**

$$
Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A high $Q$ gives a narrow, "selective" filter — useful for isolating a single murmur component. A low $Q$ gives a wide-band response that captures the full heart-sound envelope.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Noise Floor of the Preamplifier (Johnson–Nyquist)

The smallest heart sound a PCG can resolve is limited by thermal noise of the source resistance feeding the preamplifier.

$$
V_{\text{noise,rms}} \;=\; \sqrt{4 k_{\text{B}} T R \Delta f}
$$

**Numerical Evaluation Example (KTU-style):**

Given:
- $T = 310\ \text{K}$ (body temperature)
- $R = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ (typical piezo source resistance)
- $\Delta f = 1000\ \text{Hz}$ (full clinical bandwidth)
- $k_{\text{B}} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$

$$
V_{\text{noise,rms}} \;=\; \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times 5000 \times 1000}
$$

Step-by-step:
$$
= \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 5 \times 10^{3} \times 10^{3} \times 10^{-23}}
$$
$$
= \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 5 \times 10^{-17}}
$$
$$
= \sqrt{8.556 \times 10^{-15}}
$$
$$
\approx 9.25 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{V} \;=\; 0.0925\ \mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}
$$

This sets the **minimum resolvable signal** of the entire PCG chain.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PCG Transducer Technologies

| Property | **Piezoelectric (Crystal/Ceramic)** | **Electromagnetic (Dynamic)** | **Electret Condenser** |
|:---------|:------------------------------------|:-------------------------------|:----------------------|
| Operating principle | $q = dF$ (charge vs. force) | Faraday's law ($V = -d\Phi/dt$) | Capacitance change vs. SPL |
| Frequency response | Excellent (20 Hz – 10 kHz) | Limited (50 Hz – 1.5 kHz) | Wide (10 Hz – 20 kHz) |
| Sensitivity | High | Moderate | Highest |
| Output impedance | Very high (needs FET buffer) | Low | Moderate (needs preamp) |
| Power requirement | None | None | DC bias (1.5 – 9 V) |
| Cost | Low – Medium | Low | High |
| Common use | Bedside PCG, fetal monitors | Classical stethoscopes, low-cost PCG | High-fidelity electronic stethoscopes |
| Noise susceptibility | Low | Moderate | Low |
| Ruggedness | High | High | Low (sensitive to humidity) |
| **KTU Board Answer (most often used in PCG)** | ✓ **MOST PREFERRED** | Sometimes used | Premium systems |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Phonocardiograph

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **signal-flow architecture** of a clinical PCG from chest-wall acoustic pressure all the way to the display/recording device, with the synchronizing ECG path shown in parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Heart Acoustic Source] --> B1[Chest Piece with Bell and Diaphragm]
    B1 --> C1[Acoustic Coupling Medium Air Gap or Gel]
    C1 --> D1[Transducer Piezoelectric or Electret]
    D1 --> E1[Charge to Voltage Converter FET Buffer]
    E1 --> F1[Preamplifier Low Noise Instrumentation Amp]
    F1 --> G1[Band Pass Filter Bank L M1 M2 H]
    G1 --> H1[Main Amplifier with Gain Control]
    H1 --> I1[Driver Stage]
    I1 --> J1[Display Oscilloscope or Chart Recorder]

    P1[ECG Electrodes] --> P2[ECG Amplifier]
    P2 --> P3[Time Base Sync]
    P3 --> I1

    H1 --> K1[Optional Digital Path ADC plus DSP]
    K1 --> L1[PC with FFT Spectrogram Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows represent the **forward signal path** of the heart-sound channel. The path originating from the **ECG Electrodes** provides the **synchronization trace** so that the cardiologist can correlate S1 and S2 with the QRS complex and T-wave. The dashed bottom branch (right side) is the modern **digital-DSP path** that has replaced thermal chart recorders in most contemporary systems.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Preamplifier and Filter Section (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Transducer Output mV range] --> S2[Shielded Cable to Reduce EMI]
    S2 --> S3[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620 Class]
    S3 --> S4[First Stage Gain around 100]
    S4 --> S5[Buffer Op-Amp High Input Z]
    S5 --> S6[Filter Selector Switch L M1 M2 H]
    S6 --> S71[Low Filter 20 to 200 Hz RC High Pass plus Low Pass]
    S6 --> S72[Medium 1 Filter 50 to 500 Hz]
    S6 --> S73[Medium 2 Filter 100 to 800 Hz]
    S6 --> S74[High Filter 200 to 1000 Hz]
    S71 --> S8[Output Summing Amplifier]
    S72 --> S8
    S73 --> S8
    S74 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Final Power Amplifier]
    S9 --> S10[Recording Galvanometer or ADC]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Modern DSP-Based Digital Phonocardiograph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microphone Input] --> B[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[16 bit ADC at 4 kHz Sample Rate]
    C --> D[Digital Band Pass Filter via FIR]
    D --> E[FFT Engine 1024 Point]
    E --> F1[Spectrogram Display]
    E --> F2[S1 S2 Detection Algorithm]
    E --> F3[Heart Rate Calculation]
    D --> G[Storage SSD plus Cloud Upload]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why two diagrams?** The first illustrates the **classical analog PCG** (still asked in KTU board exams), and the third illustrates the **modern DSP-PCG** (emerging trend in KTU 2024 scheme questions on "recent advances in diagnostic equipment"). The second diagram is the **filter detail** that KTU examiners love to ask as a 7-mark sub-part.

---

## 4.4 Schematic of a Typical 1st-Order RC High-Pass Filter (Conceptual)

```
     C_in
  o──||──┬──────┐
          │      │
  Vin     R      │         Vout
  o───────┤      ├─────o──┤
          │      │        │
         GND    GND      (to next stage)
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU, students are often asked to *draw* the filter circuit and *label* $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$. The above symbolic sketch shows the series-capacitor / shunt-resistor topology that implements the high-pass function derived in Section 3.1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

> **Q: Define Phonocardiograph. List the standard frequency ranges of S1, S2, S3, and S4 heart sounds.**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A Phonocardiograph is a biomedical instrument used to graphically record the sounds produced by the heart, producing a tracing called a Phonocardiogram.
- **Frequency table (2 marks):**

| Heart Sound | Frequency Band |
|:-----------:|:--------------:|
| S1 | 30 – 150 Hz |
| S2 | 50 – 200 Hz |
| S3 | 20 – 70 Hz |
| S4 | 20 – 30 Hz |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

> **Q: Briefly explain the role of a piezoelectric transducer in a Phonocardiograph.**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**

- The piezoelectric transducer converts the **mechanical vibrations of the chest wall** (caused by heart sounds) into a **proportional electrical voltage** using the direct piezoelectric effect: $q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the charge coefficient and $F$ is the applied force. (2 marks)
- It acts as the **front-end sensor** of the PCG, has a wide frequency response (20 Hz to 10 kHz), and requires a **high-input-impedance FET buffer** because its source impedance is very high. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and has an internal choice. Below are **two full alternative questions** so that the student can practice either branch.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Regular – Module 3 Choice 1]**

> **Q (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional block diagram of a Phonocardiograph. List the role of each block. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Derive the transfer function and the −3 dB cutoff frequency of a first-order RC high-pass filter used to reject low-frequency body-motion artifacts in a PCG. Given $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$, compute the cutoff frequency in Hz. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

A Phonocardiograph consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Microphone / Chest Piece (1 mark):** Picks up heart sounds from the chest wall. Either a bell (for low-frequency sounds like S3, S4) or a diaphragm (for high-frequency sounds like murmurs) is used.
2. **Transducer (1 mark):** A piezoelectric or electret-condenser microphone converts acoustic pressure into a proportional electrical signal using $V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot P(t)$.
3. **Preamplifier (1 mark):** A low-noise instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620) raises the microvolt-level signal to hundreds of millivolts. CMRR > 80 dB.
4. **Band-pass Filter Bank (2 marks):** User-selectable L, M1, M2, H filters isolate the desired clinical band (20–1000 Hz).
5. **Main Amplifier and Driver (1 mark):** Boosts the signal to a level sufficient to drive a galvanometric pen motor.
6. **Display / Recorder (1 mark):** Either a thermal chart recorder, a CRO, or — in modern systems — a digital ADC + PC for FFT display. An ECG trace is recorded simultaneously for time reference.

**(b) Derivation and Numerical (7 marks)**

The transfer function derivation was shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key result is:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Substituting $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega = 10^4\ \Omega$ and $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}\ \text{F}$:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15\ \text{Hz}
$$

This filter would reject signals below ~159 Hz — suitable for the **M2 (100–800 Hz)** band selection.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Regular – Module 3 Choice 2]**

> **Q (a)** Compare piezoelectric, electromagnetic, and electret-condenser microphones for Phonocardiograph applications. Which is preferred and why? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** A series RLC band-pass filter in a PCG has $L = 100\ \text{mH}$, $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$, and $R = 50\ \Omega$. Calculate the center frequency, the −3 dB bandwidth, and the quality factor $Q$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks) — Use the Analytical Comparison Matrix from Section 3.4**

| Property | Piezoelectric | Electromagnetic | Electret Condenser |
|:---------|:--------------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| Principle | $q = dF$ | $V = -d\Phi/dt$ | $\Delta C$ vs. SPL |
| Sensitivity | High | Moderate | Highest |
| Frequency range | Wide | Limited | Widest |
| Power | None | None | DC bias required |
| Preferred for PCG | ✓ **Yes** | Sometimes | High-end systems |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The **piezoelectric microphone is preferred** for most clinical PCGs because it offers high sensitivity, a wide frequency response (20 Hz – 10 kHz) covering all heart sounds, requires no external power, and is rugged. The electret condenser is reserved for premium digital PCGs where the highest fidelity is required.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**Center Frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{100 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10^{-7}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.162 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 503.3\ \text{Hz}
$$

**−3 dB Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta f = \frac{R}{2\pi L} = \frac{50}{2\pi \times 0.1} = \frac{50}{0.6283} \approx 79.58\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Quality Factor (2 marks):**

$$
Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f} = \frac{503.3}{79.58} \approx 6.32
$$

**Verification (1 mark):** Using the alternative formula $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{50}\sqrt{\frac{0.1}{10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{50}\sqrt{10^5} = \frac{316.23}{50} \approx 6.32$ ✓

A $Q$ of ~6.3 is appropriate for the M1 filter band (50–500 Hz) in clinical PCGs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where B.Tech Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in PCG Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the ECG synchronizing path** in the block diagram. KTU examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark** for the synchronizing trace — missing it costs a full grade point.
> 2. **Confusing the bell and diaphragm** — the *bell* picks up **low frequencies (S3, S4, mid-diastolic murmurs)**, the *diaphragm* picks up **high frequencies (S1, S2, systolic murmurs, rubs)**. Reversing this is a guaranteed half-mark loss.
> 3. **Using $f_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ instead of $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$** — a single missed factor of $2\pi$ is the most common numerical error.
> 4. **Writing the unit of $Q$ as Hz** — Quality factor is **dimensionless**.
> 5. **Omitting the −3 dB justification** in filter derivations. KTU specifically requires the statement *"the cutoff is the frequency at which the output power is half (−3 dB)"* to award full marks.
> 6. **Stating that the PCG uses an "audio speaker" as transducer** — this is wrong; the transducer is a **microphone**, which converts sound *to* electricity (not vice versa).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section on a single index card for last-hour revision.**

- [x] **Phonocardiograph = Acoustic → Electrical → Graphical** recording of heart sounds.
- [x] Heart sound bands: **S1 (30–150 Hz), S2 (50–200 Hz), S3 (20–70 Hz), S4 (20–30 Hz), Murmurs (20–1000 Hz)**.
- [x] Six functional blocks: **Pickup → Transducer → Preamplifier → Filter Bank → Main Amplifier → Recorder**.
- [x] Preferred transducer: **Piezoelectric (high sensitivity, no power, wide bandwidth)**.
- [x] Bell = low frequency; Diaphragm = high frequency.
- [x] ECG is always recorded in parallel for **time synchronization** with S1 and S2.
- [x] Filter cutoff: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
- [x] Band-pass center: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$.
- [x] Quality factor: $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ (dimensionless).
- [x] Thermal noise floor: $V_{\text{noise}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$.
- [x] Gain in dB: $G_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}\right)$.
- [x] Modern PCGs use **DSP + FFT** for spectrogram display and automatic S1/S2 detection.
- [x] Clinical PCG bandwidth: **20 Hz – 1000 Hz**; pediatric extended to 2 kHz.
- [x] Standard stethoscope bell diameter: **25–35 mm**; diaphragm: **40–50 mm**.
- [x] Remember the factor **$2\pi$** in every cutoff and resonant-frequency expression — this is the most common KTU board trap.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patient Monitoring Systems: ECG, NIBP, PPG, and Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT504 - ANALYTICAL AND DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Patient Monitoring Systems: ECG, NIBP, PPG, and Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Patient Monitoring System (PMS) — Formal Definition
A **Patient Monitoring System (PMS)** is a cluster of biomedical instruments that continuously or intermittently measure, display, record, and alarm a patient's vital physiological parameters. The four cornerstone modalities covered in the KTU Module-3 syllabus are the **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** for cardiac electrical activity, **Non-Invasive Blood Pressure (NIBP)** for hemodynamic status, **Photoplethysmogram (PPG)** for peripheral pulse and oxygen saturation, and **Temperature** for thermoregulatory status.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module is *parameter-centric* (ECG, NIBP, PPG, Temperature) — emphasis is on **transducer/electrode physics**, **signal-conditioning blocks**, and **clinical waveform interpretation**, not on full hospital IT/networking protocols.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Dashboard"
Think of a Patient Monitor as the **dashboard of a moving car**:

| Car Dashboard | Patient Monitor |
|---------------|-----------------|
| Speedometer (km/h) | **HR (bpm)** from ECG |
| Oil-pressure gauge | **NIBP (mmHg)** |
| Fuel-level indicator | **SpO2 (%)** from PPG |
| Engine temperature lamp | **Body Temperature (°C)** |
| Check-engine alarm | **Audible/Visual Alarms** |

Just as a dashboard translates hidden mechanical states into readable numbers, a PMS translates invisible bioelectric, hemodynamic, optical, and thermal phenomena into clinician-readable signals.

### 1.3 Clinical Parameter Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physiological Ranges (Adult, Resting):**
> - Heart Rate (HR) = **60 – 100 bpm**
> - Systolic BP = **90 – 120 mmHg**, Diastolic BP = **60 – 80 mmHg**
> - SpO2 (arterial oxygen saturation) = **95 – 100 %**
> - Core Body Temperature = **36.1 – 37.2 °C** (97 – 99 °F)

### 1.4 Why These Four Parameters?
They form the **"Vital Four"** because together they characterize the **Cardiovascular-Respiratory-Thermal axis** of a patient — sufficient to triage > 80 % of bedside emergencies (shock, hypoxia, arrhythmia, hypothermia, sepsis) before invasive lines are placed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Electrocardiogram (ECG)

#### 2.1.1 Origin of the ECG Signal
The heart is a **synchronised electrical pump**. The depolarisation–repolarisation wave travelling through the myocardium generates an electric field vector that can be sensed on the body surface. The voltage picked up between two skin electrodes is the **electrocardiogram**.

- Source: SA node → atria → AV node → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → ventricles.
- Amplitude at body surface: **0.5 mV – 4 mV** (typically ~1 mV).
- Frequency content: **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** (diagnostic ECG: 0.05 – 100 Hz; monitor: 0.5 – 40 Hz).

#### 2.1.2 The ECG Waveform and Its Meaning

| Wave / Segment | Physiological Origin | Typical Duration | Clinical Use |
|----------------|---------------------|------------------|--------------|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarisation | < 120 ms | Atrial enlargement |
| **PR interval** | Atrial depolarisation + AV nodal delay | 120 – 200 ms | AV-block detection |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarisation | 80 – 120 ms | Bundle-branch blocks, Q-wave MI |
| **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (isoelectric) | 80 – 120 ms | Ischaemia, infarction |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarisation | 160 ms | Electrolyte disturbances |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular activity | 350 – 440 ms | Drug-toxicity, SCD risk |

#### 2.1.3 ECG Lead Systems
- **Bipolar (3-lead):** Lead I (LA–RA), Lead II (LL–RA), Lead III (LL–LA). Einthoven's triangle.
- **Augmented unipolar (5-lead):** aVR, aVL, aVF + V-lead.
- **Precordial (12-lead):** V1 – V6 placed across the chest in standard intercostal positions.
- **Mason-Likar (modified) limb leads:** used during exercise stress testing to minimise artefact.

**Einthoven's Law:**

$$\text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}$$

#### 2.1.4 Heart-Rate Calculation (ECG Paper Method)
Standard ECG paper speed: **25 mm/s**.
- 1 small box = 1 mm = **0.04 s**.
- 1 large box = 5 mm = **0.20 s**.

If N = number of large boxes between two consecutive R-waves:

$$\text{HR (bpm)} = \frac{1500}{\text{N (small boxes)}} = \frac{300}{\text{N (large boxes)}}$$

#### 2.1.5 ECG Front-End Block Diagram
Electrode → **Lead Selector** → **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** → **Isolation Barrier (patient safety)** → **Band-Pass Filter (0.05 – 150 Hz)** → **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz mains)** → **A/D Converter** → **Display/Alarm**.

> [!NOTE]
> The INA must have:
> - CMRR ≥ **100 dB** (50/60 Hz rejection)
> - Input impedance ≥ **10 MΩ** (to avoid loading the skin)
> - Noise ≤ **5 μV** (RTI) at 0.1 – 100 Hz

---

### 2.2 Non-Invasive Blood Pressure (NIBP)

#### 2.2.1 What is Blood Pressure?
**Blood Pressure (BP)** is the lateral force per unit area exerted by the blood on the vessel wall. It is reported as two numbers:

$$\text{BP} = \frac{\text{Systolic}}{\text{Diastolic}} \;\; (\text{mmHg})$$

- **Systolic (SBP):** peak pressure during ventricular contraction (ejection phase).
- **Diastolic (DBP):** trough pressure during ventricular relaxation.
- **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP):** perfusion pressure of organs.

$$\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \frac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP})$$

#### 2.2.2 The Oscillometric Method (Most Common in PMS)
The cuff is inflated above SBP and slowly deflated. Tiny pressure oscillations (≈ 1 – 5 mmHg) superimposed on the cuff pressure are detected by a **piezo-resistive / capacitive transducer**. As cuff pressure falls, oscillation amplitude first rises to a **maximum at MAP**, then falls.

- **MAP** = cuff pressure at *maximum oscillation amplitude*.
- **SBP** = cuff pressure at the *first detectable rise* in oscillations (typically ~50 % of max amplitude on the rising side).
- **DBP** = cuff pressure at the *last detectable oscillation* (typically ~70 % of max amplitude on the falling side).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Oscillometric devices **measure MAP directly** and *algorithmically estimate* SBP and DBP — that is why they need empirical amplitude-ratio tables per manufacturer.

#### 2.2.3 The Auscultatory Method (Riva-Rocci + Korotkoff)
- A stethoscope over the brachial artery listens for **Korotkoff sounds** as the cuff deflates.
- **Phase I** (first tap) → SBP; **Phase V** (disappearance) → DBP.

#### 2.2.4 Cuff Sizing Rule
Cuff bladder width must be **40 %** of arm circumference (and length ≥ 80 % of circumference). A too-narrow cuff falsely elevates BP; a too-wide cuff falsely depresses BP.

---

### 2.3 Photoplethysmography (PPG)

#### 2.3.1 Definition
**PPG** is an optical, non-invasive technique that detects **changes in blood volume** in the microvascular bed of tissue (typically fingertip, earlobe, or toe) by shining light into the skin and measuring the transmitted/reflected intensity.

#### 2.3.2 Optical Principle
- **Source:** LED at two wavelengths — **Red (660 nm)** and **Infrared (940 nm)**.
- **Detector:** photodiode / phototransistor.
- **Beer-Lambert Law of light absorption** in tissue:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\varepsilon(\lambda) \, c \, l}$$

where ε(λ) is the wavelength-dependent absorption coefficient. **Oxygenated haemoglobin (HbO2)** absorbs more IR, while **deoxygenated haemoglobin (Hb)** absorbs more red. This wavelength-dependent absorption is the basis of **pulse oximetry (SpO2)**.

#### 2.3.3 The PPG Waveform
- **AC component** (pulsatile, ~1–5 % of total) — synchronised with each heartbeat, due to arterial blood volume changes.
- **DC component** (baseline, ~95–99 % of total) — due to venous blood, skin, bone, and other static tissue.

#### 2.3.4 SpO2 — The "Ratio of Ratios"

$$\text{SpO}_2 \;(\%) = \frac{AC_{660} / DC_{660}}{AC_{940} / DC_{940}} \times 100$$

The ratio is calibrated empirically against co-oximetry on healthy volunteers down to ~70 % saturation.

#### 2.3.5 Transmission vs Reflection PPG
| Mode | Path | Typical Site |
|------|------|--------------|
| **Transmission** | Light passes completely through tissue (LED ↔ PD on opposite sides) | Fingertip, earlobe, toe |
| **Reflection (Backscatter)** | Light scatters back from tissue to detector on the same side | Forehead, wrist, nasal septum |

---

### 2.4 Temperature Measurement

#### 2.4.1 Why Body Temperature?
Core temperature is a tightly regulated (~ ± 0.2 °C) indicator of metabolic, infectious, and thermoregulatory status.

#### 2.4.2 Temperature Measurement Sites
- **Core:** pulmonary artery, oesophagus, tympanic membrane, urinary bladder.
- **Peripheral / Clinical:** oral (sublingual), axillary, rectal, skin (forehead), tympanic (ear).

#### 2.4.3 Temperature Transducers Used in PMS

| Sensor | Principle | Range | Accuracy | Speed |
|--------|-----------|-------|----------|-------|
| **Thermistor (NTC)** | Resistance falls exponentially with T | -50 to 150 °C | ± 0.1 °C | Fast (~1 s) |
| **RTD (Pt100 / Pt1000)** | Resistance rises linearly with T | -200 to 850 °C | ± 0.3 °C (Class A: ± 0.15 °C) | Slow (~3–5 s) |
| **Thermocouple (Type T / K)** | Seebeck EMF between two metals | -270 to 1370 °C | ± 1 °C | Very fast (ms) |
| **Infrared (tympanic / temporal)** | Planck's law of blackbody radiation | -70 to 380 °C | ± 0.2 °C | < 1 s |
| **Semiconductor (LM35, TMP36)** | Band-gap voltage proportional to T | -55 to 150 °C | ± 0.5 °C | Fast |

#### 2.4.4 The Thermistor Equation (Steinhart–Hart)

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C \left[\ln(R)\right]^3$$

where $T$ is in Kelvin and $R$ in ohms, A, B, C are calibration constants.

A simpler two-point exponential model is often used:

$$R(T) = R_0 \, e^{\,B\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where $R_0$ is the resistance at reference $T_0$ (usually 25 °C = 298.15 K) and B is the **β-constant** (typically 3000 – 5000 K for medical NTC thermistors).

#### 2.4.5 Unit Conversions (must be memorised for KTU)

$$^\circ F = \frac{9}{5}\,^\circ C + 32, \qquad ^\circ C = \frac{5}{9}(^\circ F - 32), \qquad K = ^\circ C + 273.15$$

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---------------|---------|---------|
| 1 | $\text{HR} = 300 / N_{\text{large box}}$ | Heart rate from ECG paper | ECG |
| 2 | $\text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}$ | Einthoven's law | ECG |
| 3 | $\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \tfrac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP})$ | Mean arterial pressure | NIBP |
| 4 | $R = \frac{\Delta P}{Q}$ | Vascular resistance (Ohm's law analogue) | NIBP |
| 5 | $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | Beer–Lambert absorption | PPG / SpO2 |
| 6 | $\text{SpO}_2 \propto \dfrac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$ | Ratio of ratios | PPG |
| 7 | $R(T) = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | NTC thermistor model | Temperature |
| 8 | $1/T = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | Steinhart–Hart equation | Temperature |
| 9 | $W_{\text{B-V}}(\lambda)$ | Wavelength-dependent absorption (Hb/HbO2) | PPG |
| 10 | $^\circ F = 9/5 \;^\circ C + 32$ | Unit conversion | Temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of these equations:** The Einthoven law is the *first example* in biomedical engineering of using a **reference-frame vector sum** — the same mathematical structure that underlies modern 12-lead vectorcardiography. The MAP equation is *Poiseuille's-law* in time-averaged form, used in *fluid resuscitation* protocols. The Beer-Lambert model underpins **fNIRS** (functional near-infrared spectroscopy) for brain imaging. The thermistor equation is the basis of every **incubator, dialysis machine, and warm-air blanket** controller in a hospital.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heart Rate from ECG Paper

**Statement:** Given an ECG tracing moving at the standard 25 mm/s, derive the formula $\text{HR (bpm)} = 300 / N_{\text{large box}}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the constants of ECG paper.**
At a chart speed $v = 25$ mm/s:

$$1 \text{ small box} = 1 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{\text{small}} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} = 0.04 \text{ s}$$

$$1 \text{ large box} = 5 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{\text{large}} = 5 \times 0.04 = 0.20 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Express one R–R interval in seconds.**
If the R–R interval spans $N$ large boxes:

$$T_{\text{R-R}} = N \times t_{\text{large}} = 0.20 N \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Convert period to frequency (bpm).**
Heart rate in beats per second = $1 / T_{\text{R-R}}$. Multiply by 60 to convert to beats per minute:

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{R-R}}} = \frac{60}{0.20 N} = \frac{300}{N} \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent form using small boxes.**
Since $N_{\text{large}} = N_{\text{small}} / 5$:

$$\text{HR} = \frac{300}{N_{\text{small}}/5} = \frac{1500}{N_{\text{small}}} \text{ bpm}$$

> **Numerical example:** If the R–R interval covers 4 large boxes: HR = 300 / 4 = **75 bpm** (normal).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)

**Statement:** Derive the formula $\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \tfrac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP})$.

**Step 1 — Model the arterial pressure waveform.**
Approximate the aortic pressure as a **trapezoidal** waveform: a linear rise from DBP to SBP during the *systolic ejection* phase (occupying roughly $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of the cardiac cycle) and a linear fall from SBP back to DBP during *diastole* ($\tfrac{2}{3}$ of the cycle).

**Step 2 — Compute the time-averaged pressure over one cycle $T$.**
The integral of $P(t)$ over one cardiac cycle gives the area under the curve, which equals $\text{MAP} \times T$.

**Step 3 — Geometric decomposition.**
The area can be split into a **rectangle** of height DBP (width = T) plus a **triangle** of height (SBP – DBP) and base T (the systolic rise + diastolic fall form a triangle symmetric about the peak).

$$\text{Area} = \text{DBP} \times T + \frac{1}{2} \times (SBP - DBP) \times T$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the cycle period $T$.**

$$\text{MAP} = \frac{\text{Area}}{T} = \text{DBP} + \frac{1}{2}(SBP - DBP)$$

**Step 5 — Empirical correction.**
Because diastole actually occupies $\sim \tfrac{2}{3}$ (not $\tfrac{1}{2}$) of the cycle, the triangle's *weight* is replaced by the empirical factor $\tfrac{1}{3}$ (from weighted integration over the asymmetric cycle):

$$\boxed{\;\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)\;}$$

> **Numerical example:** For BP = 120 / 80 mmHg: MAP = 80 + ⅓(40) = 80 + 13.3 = **93.3 mmHg**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — SpO2 via the Ratio of Ratios

**Statement:** Show why a **single-wavelength** PPG cannot yield SpO2, but the **ratio of AC/DC at two wavelengths** eliminates common-mode errors.

**Step 1 — Light reaching the photodetector.**
The Beer–Lambert law gives the transmitted intensity through a tissue layer of optical path length $l(t)$:

$$I(\lambda, t) = I_0(\lambda) \, e^{-\varepsilon_{\text{HbO2}}(\lambda) \, c_{\text{HbO2}} \, l(t) - \varepsilon_{\text{Hb}}(\lambda) \, c_{\text{Hb}} \, l(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the two components of $l(t)$.**
Let $l(t) = l_0 + \Delta l(t)$, where $l_0$ is the *static* path length (DC) and $\Delta l(t)$ is the *pulsatile* path length change (AC) due to arterial blood volume change.

**Step 3 — Expand the exponential using Taylor series (small $\Delta l$):**

$$I(\lambda,t) \approx \underbrace{I_0 e^{-\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} l_0}}_{DC} \; - \; \underbrace{I_0 \, \varepsilon_{\text{eff}} \, \Delta l(t) \, e^{-\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} l_0}}_{AC}$$

**Step 4 — Form the AC/DC ratio (normalised modulation depth).**

$$\frac{AC(\lambda)}{DC(\lambda)} = \varepsilon_{\text{eff}}(\lambda) \, \Delta l(t)$$

**Step 5 — Take the ratio of two wavelengths to cancel $\Delta l(t)$.**
At red ($\lambda_R$ = 660 nm) and infrared ($\lambda_{IR}$ = 940 nm):

$$\Phi = \frac{AC(\lambda_R) / DC(\lambda_R)}{AC(\lambda_{IR}) / DC(\lambda_{IR})} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{HbO2}}(\lambda_R) S + \varepsilon_{\text{Hb}}(\lambda_R)(1-S)}{\varepsilon_{\text{HbO2}}(\lambda_{IR}) S + \varepsilon_{\text{Hb}}(\lambda_{IR})(1-S)}$$

where $S$ is the fractional oxygen saturation. Since the absorption coefficients are **known constants**, $\Phi$ is a **monotonic function of $S$** — measured empirically on volunteers using a co-oximeter as the gold standard.

$$\boxed{\;\text{SpO}_2 \;(\%) = f(\Phi) \times 100\;}$$

> **Clinical insight:** This is exactly why pulse oximeters are *inaccurate at low perfusion* (cold finger, hypotension) — the AC term becomes so small that it is buried in noise. The ratio-of-ratios method **cannot** correct for *motion artefact*, which is why modern pulse oximeters use **adaptive filtering** (Masimo SET technology).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — NIBP Oscillometric Amplitude Ratios

**Statement:** Show that the cuff pressure at *maximum oscillation amplitude* corresponds to **MAP**.

**Step 1 — Pressure balance across the arterial wall.**
When the cuff pressure $P_c$ is greater than SBP, the artery is occluded → no pulsation reaches the cuff. When $P_c$ falls below DBP, the artery is fully open and its wall is *flaccid* → pulsations again become small. Maximum wall *compliance* (and hence maximum transmission of the pulse to the cuff) occurs when the transmural pressure (intra-arterial − cuff pressure) is **near zero**, i.e. when cuff pressure ≈ intra-arterial mean pressure.

**Step 2 — Mathematical model.**
The transmission coefficient $T(P_c)$ from artery to cuff is maximised when the arterial wall is most compliant. For a thin-walled elastic tube, compliance $C(P_c) = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}$ peaks when $P_{\text{artery}} = P_c$. The time-averaged intra-arterial pressure is by definition **MAP**.

**Step 3 — Algorithm for SBP and DBP.**
Empirically, SBP and DBP are identified at the *rising* and *falling* cuff pressures where the oscillation amplitude equals a fixed fraction of the maximum:

$$\text{At SBP: } \quad A(P_c) = k_S \cdot A_{\max}, \quad k_S \approx 0.5$$
$$\text{At DBP: } \quad A(P_c) = k_D \cdot A_{\max}, \quad k_D \approx 0.7 \text{ to } 0.85$$

(These empirical coefficients are **proprietary** to each manufacturer — Philips, GE, Omron use different values, which is why inter-device readings may differ by ± 5 mmHg.)

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — NTC Thermistor Beta Equation

**Statement:** Derive the exponential thermistor model $R(T) = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$.

**Step 1 — Conduction in a semiconductor.**
In an NTC thermistor, charge carriers (electrons/holes) are thermally excited across the band gap. The intrinsic carrier concentration is:

$$n_i \propto T^{3/2} \, e^{-E_g / 2k_B T}$$

**Step 2 — Resistivity is inversely proportional to $n_i$:**

$$\rho(T) \propto e^{E_g / 2k_B T}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio at two temperatures $T$ and $T_0$:**

$$\frac{R(T)}{R(T_0)} = \frac{\rho(T)}{\rho(T_0)} = \frac{e^{E_g / 2k_B T}}{e^{E_g / 2k_B T_0}} = e^{\,B\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where the β-constant $B = E_g / 2k_B$. For medical NTCs, $B \in [3000, 5000]$ K.

$$\boxed{\;R(T) = R_0 \, e^{\,B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Inverse (solve for T given R):**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{B} \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right)$$

> **Numerical example:** A thermistor has $R_0 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ at 25 °C, $B = 3950$ K. Find $R$ at 37 °C.
> $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $T = 310.15$ K.
> $R = 10000 \cdot e^{3950(1/310.15 - 1/298.15)} = 10000 \cdot e^{3950 \times (-0.0001297)} = 10000 \cdot e^{-0.5124} = 10000 \times 0.599 = \mathbf{5.99 \;k\Omega}$.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Choice for Temperature

| Sensor | Sensitivity | Linearity | Self-heating | Best Application in PMS |
|--------|-------------|-----------|--------------|--------------------------|
| NTC Thermistor | High (-4 %/°C) | Non-linear (exponential) | Significant at low current | Skin, tympanic, axillary probes |
| Pt100 RTD | Medium (0.385 Ω/°C) | Excellent (linear) | Low | Continuous core-temp monitoring (e.g. ICU bladder probe) |
| Type-T Thermocouple | Low (40 μV/°C) | Fair (needs ice-bath / cold-junction comp.) | Negligible | Fast-response rectal/esophageal |
| IR (Thermopile) | Medium | Calibrated linear | None (non-contact) | Tympanic, temporal-artery scanners |
| LM35 IC | Medium (10 mV/°C) | Excellent (linear) | Negligible | General-purpose skin patches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **β-constant** $B$ with the *thermistor dissipation constant* $\delta$ (mW/°C) — these are **two different parameters**. $B$ describes temperature sensitivity; $\delta$ describes the *self-heating* error due to the measurement current.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Patient Monitor Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Patient Body Surface] --> B1[ECG Electrodes]
    A --> B2[NIBP Cuff + Transducer]
    A --> B3[PPG SpO2 Probe]
    A --> B4[Temp Probe]

    B1 --> C1[Lead Selector]
    C1 --> D1[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    D1 --> E1[Band-Pass Filter 0.05-150 Hz]
    E1 --> F1[50/60 Hz Notch Filter]
    F1 --> G1[Isolation Barrier]
    G1 --> H1[12-bit ADC]

    B2 --> C2[Cuff Pump + Dump Valve]
    C2 --> D2[Pressure Transducer]
    D2 --> E2[Oscillation Amplifier]
    E2 --> F2[MAP Algorithm MCU]
    F2 --> H1

    B3 --> C3[Red LED 660 nm]
    B3 --> C4[IR LED 940 nm]
    C3 --> D3[Photodiode Detector]
    C4 --> D3
    D3 --> E3[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    E3 --> F3[AC DC Separator + Ratio Calc]
    F3 --> H1

    B4 --> C5[Wheatstone Bridge]
    C5 --> D4[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    D4 --> E4[Linearization LUT]
    E4 --> H1

    H1 --> I[Microcontroller DSP]
    I --> J1[LCD Touch Display]
    I --> J2[Audible Visual Alarm]
    I --> J3[Network HIS EMR Gateway]
    I --> J4[Trend Storage SD Card]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    style I fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    style J1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
    style J2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#fad7a0,stroke:#7e5109,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — NIBP Oscillometric Subsystem

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[DC Motor Pump] --> M1[Cuff Bladder]
    M1 --> V1[Solenoid Dump Valve]
    M1 --> T1[Piezoresistive Pressure Transducer]
    T1 --> IA1[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    IA1 --> HPF1[High-Pass Filter 0.5 Hz]
    HPF1 --> ADC1[12-bit ADC]
    ADC1 --> MCU1[Microcontroller]
    MCU1 --> P1
    MCU1 --> V1
    MCU1 --> DSP1[Oscillation Peak Detection]
    DSP1 --> MAP1[MAP Detection]
    DSP1 --> SDP1[SBP DBP Algorithm]
    MAP1 --> LCD1[Display mmHg]
    SDP1 --> LCD1

    style MCU1 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    style LCD1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
```

    CLK1 --> DRV2[IR LED Driver 940 nm]

    AMP1 --> ADC2[ADC]
    ADC2 --> RATIO1[Ratio of Ratios Calculator]
    RATIO1 --> LUT1[Empirical SpO2 Lookup Table]
    LUT1 --> DSP2[Display + Alarm]

    style CLK1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    style RATIO1 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Note:** Modern ICs (e.g., Texas Instruments **ADS1292**, **AFE4400** for SpO2) integrate most of the blocks above into a *single chip*, but the *functional chain* remains identical. The Mermaid schematics above map the **interactions** between transducer, signal-conditioning, algorithm, and display blocks — this is what the KTU examiner expects in 14-mark block-diagram questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Q1. (3 Marks)**
> **Define the Electrocardiogram (ECG) and list the standard amplitude and frequency ranges of the ECG signal.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is the graphical recording of the electrical activity of the heart, obtained by placing electrodes on the body surface. It represents the time-varying potential differences generated by the depolarisation and repolarisation of cardiac muscle.

- **Amplitude range:** **0.5 mV to 4 mV** (typical QRS peak ≈ 1 mV).
- **Frequency range:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** for full diagnostic ECG; **0.5 Hz to 40 Hz** for monitoring.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024] Q2. (3 Marks)**
> **Explain the principle of the oscillometric method of non-invasive blood pressure measurement.**

**Model Answer:**
The **oscillometric method** measures BP by detecting **tiny pressure oscillations** in an inflated cuff as it is slowly deflated. The cuff pressure is monitored by a piezo-resistive transducer. The amplitude of these oscillations (1–5 mmHg) varies with cuff pressure. The cuff pressure at which oscillation amplitude is **maximum** corresponds to the **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)**. **Systolic** and **diastolic** pressures are algorithmically identified at fixed amplitude ratios (≈ 50 % rising, ≈ 70 % falling) of the maximum oscillation. This is the most widely used technique in modern patient monitors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Q3 (A). (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a single-channel ECG machine. Describe the function of each block.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the heart rate from an ECG strip in which the R–R interval measures 18 small boxes. Convert the answer to period in seconds.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

A single-channel ECG machine converts the heart's bioelectric activity into a calibrated visual trace. The functional block chain is:

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl disposable) + Gel Interface** — provide low-impedance (< 5 kΩ) electrical contact with the skin.
2. **Lead Selector** — switches between Leads I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF (and V-leads for 12-lead).
3. **Patient Cable & Defibrillator Protection** — series resistors + gas-discharge tubes + Zener diodes protect the patient and the front-end from defibrillator shocks (~5 kV).
4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — typically AD620 / INA128. Gain = 10; CMRR > 100 dB; input impedance > 10 MΩ.
5. **Isolation Barrier** — optical or transformer isolation to limit patient leakage current below **10 μA** (Type CF — cardiac floating).
6. **Band-Pass Filter** — 0.05 Hz high-pass (blocks DC electrode potential) to 150 Hz low-pass (filters muscle artefact and EMI).
7. **50/60 Hz Notch Filter** — twin-T or adaptive notch to reject mains hum.
8. **Preamplifier (gain ≈ 1000)** — boosts the ~1 mV signal to ~1 V.
9. **A/D Converter** — 12-bit, ≥ 500 samples/s/channel.
10. **Display / Printer / Storage** — LCD, thermal-array recorder, SD card.

**Key engineering numbers (must memorise):**
- Patient leakage current: < **10 μA** (CF), < **100 μA** (BF).
- Defibrillator recovery time: < **5 s** to 95 % baseline.
- Gain: ~1000 overall; 1 mV input → 1 cm pen deflection (calibration pulse).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** R–R interval = 18 small boxes.
**Step 1 — Convert small boxes to large boxes:**

$$N_{\text{large}} = \frac{N_{\text{small}}}{5} = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6 \text{ large boxes}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the heart-rate formula:**

$$\text{HR} = \frac{300}{N_{\text{large}}} = \frac{300}{3.6} = 83.33 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent using small boxes:**

$$\text{HR} = \frac{1500}{18} = 83.33 \text{ bpm} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute the period in seconds:**

$$T_{\text{R-R}} = 18 \times 0.04 = 0.72 \text{ s}$$

**Verification:** $\text{HR} = 60 / 0.72 = 83.33$ bpm ✓

**Answer:** HR = **83.3 bpm**, Period = **0.72 s**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Q3 (B). (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice to Q3A)*
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **pulse oximeter** (PPG-based SpO2 measurement).
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A patient has a heart rate measured by ECG as 72 bpm. Calculate the R–R interval in (i) seconds, (ii) milliseconds, and (iii) number of large ECG boxes. Also compute the MAP if BP = 130 / 85 mmHg.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

A **pulse oximeter** is a non-invasive device that measures the percentage of **arterial oxygen saturation (SpO2)** in peripheral blood using PPG. Block chain:

1. **Dual-Wavelength LED Driver:** Time-multiplexed **660 nm (red)** and **940 nm (IR)** LEDs illuminate the finger (or earlobe).
2. **Photodiode Detector:** Receives transmitted light and converts to current.
3. **Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA):** Converts nA-level photocurrent to a usable voltage.
4. **Demultiplexer + Sample-and-Hold:** Separates the red and IR channels.
5. **AC / DC Separator:** AC = pulsatile (arterial) component; DC = baseline (venous + tissue) component.
6. **Ratio Calculator:** Computes $\Phi = \dfrac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$.
7. **Empirical Lookup Table:** $\Phi \rightarrow$ SpO2 (%) (calibrated against co-oximeter).
8. **Display + Alarm:** SpO2 value, plethysmographic waveform, and alarm if SpO2 < 90 %.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — R–R interval in seconds:**

$$T_{\text{R-R}} = \frac{60}{\text{HR}} = \frac{60}{72} = 0.833 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — In milliseconds:**

$$T_{\text{R-R}} = 0.833 \times 1000 = 833 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — In large ECG boxes:**
At 25 mm/s, 1 large box = 0.20 s.

$$N_{\text{large}} = \frac{0.833}{0.20} = 4.167 \text{ large boxes}$$

**Step 4 — Mean Arterial Pressure:**

$$\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \frac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP}) = 85 + \frac{1}{3}(130 - 85)$$

$$\text{MAP} = 85 + \frac{1}{3}(45) = 85 + 15 = \mathbf{100 \text{ mmHg}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **ECG frequency range** — Students often write "0.5 – 50 Hz". The correct diagnostic range is **0.05 – 150 Hz**. Examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark for this.
> 2. **MAP formula confusion** — Some students write MAP = (SBP + DBP) / 2. The correct *empirical* formula is **DBP + ⅓(SBP–DBP)**, which accounts for the longer diastolic phase. Marks lost: 1 – 2.
> 3. **SpO2 ratio direction** — Students invert the ratio. Always: **RED ratio (660) over IR ratio (940)**, because Hb absorbs more red and HbO2 absorbs more IR. Higher Φ → lower SpO2.
> 4. **Cuff sizing rule** — Writing "cuff should be the same as arm length" instead of *"cuff bladder width = 40 % of arm circumference"* loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Bipolar vs Unipolar leads** — Confusing Lead I/II/III (bipolar) with aVR/aVL/aVF (augmented unipolar). The "a" stands for "augmented", not "arterial".
> 6. **Units of β-constant** — Always in **Kelvin**, never °C. Marks lost: 1.
> 7. **Skipping the calibration step** in pulse oximeter block diagrams. KTU expects the **empirical lookup table** in the answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ECG amplitude** = 0.5 – 4 mV; **frequency** = 0.05 – 150 Hz. PQRSTU waves, **PR interval = 120 – 200 ms**, **QRS = 80 – 120 ms**, **QT = 350 – 440 ms**.
- **Einthoven's Law:** Lead II = Lead I + Lead III.
- **HR formula:** $\text{HR} = 300/N_{\text{large}} = 1500/N_{\text{small}}$ bpm. ECG paper speed = **25 mm/s**.
- **MAP** = DBP + ⅓(SBP − DBP). MAP **< 65 mmHg** → inadequate organ perfusion (septic shock).
- **NIBP oscillometric method** measures **MAP directly**; SBP/DBP are *algorithmic* estimates using amplitude ratios ~0.5 (rising) and ~0.7 (falling).
- **Korotkoff Phase I** = SBP; **Phase V** = DBP (auscultatory method).
- **Cuff bladder width = 40 % of arm circumference**; **length ≥ 80 %** of circumference.
- **PPG** uses **660 nm (red) + 940 nm (IR)**. **SpO2** uses the **ratio of ratios** to cancel common-mode errors (motion, ambient light).
- **PPG has AC + DC components.** AC = arterial pulsatile (~1–5 % of total), DC = venous + tissue baseline.
- **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ — basis of all optical oximetry and fNIRS.
- **Body temperature transducers:** NTC thermistor (β ≈ 3950 K), Pt100 RTD (0.385 Ω/°C), Type T thermocouple, IR thermopile.
- **NTC equation:** $R(T) = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$; **Steinhart-Hart** is the more accurate 3-parameter form.
- **Temperature conversions:** °F = 9/5 °C + 32; K = °C + 273.15.
- **Patient safety:** leakage current < **10 μA (Type CF)**, < **100 μA (Type BF)**. Always mention this in any ECG/monitor design question.
- **CMRR** of ECG INA must be **> 100 dB**; **input impedance > 10 MΩ**; **noise < 5 μV RTI**.
- **Defibrillator protection** is a *mandatory* block in any ECG front-end — series resistors, gas-discharge tubes, Zener clamps.
- **Calibration pulse** of 1 mV is *internally* generated and printed on every clinical ECG trace for legal/regulatory traceability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cardiac Stress Testing and Arrhythmia Monitors: Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT504 - ANALYTICAL AND DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cardiac Stress Testing and Arrhythmia Monitors: Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cardiac Stress Testing

### Formal Academic Definition
**Cardiac Stress Testing** (also known as **Exercise Stress Test**, **Treadmill Test (TMT)**, or **Exercise Electrocardiography**) is a non-invasive diagnostic procedure used in clinical cardiology to evaluate the functional capacity of the cardiovascular system under controlled physical stress. The patient is subjected to graded physical exercise (typically on a motorized treadmill or a cycle ergometer) while the **electrocardiogram (ECG)**, **blood pressure**, and **heart rate** are continuously monitored. The procedure is governed by standardized protocols such as the **Bruce Protocol**, the **Modified Bruce Protocol**, or the **Naughton Protocol**, each prescribing a defined progression of speed and grade for the treadmill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Cardiac stress testing is critical in diagnosing **Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)**, assessing **exercise-induced myocardial ischemia**, evaluating **arrhythmia threshold**, and determining **functional capacity** post-myocardial infarction. The standard indicator of a positive stress test is **≥ 1 mm of horizontal or down-sloping ST-segment depression** measured **80 ms after the J-point**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the heart as a car's engine being tested on a dynamometer. When the car is at rest (idle), the engine may appear perfectly normal even if there is a hidden mechanical fault. However, when you push the engine to higher RPMs (stress), the latent fault — say, a fuel line blockage — reveals itself through poor performance, knocking sounds, or abnormal emissions. Similarly, a heart with partially blocked coronary arteries may function normally at rest, but when stressed by exercise, the limited blood supply fails to meet the increased oxygen demand, producing characteristic **ST-segment depressions** and **T-wave inversions** in the ECG. The stress test is therefore a controlled "engine test" for the heart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physiological Benchmarks (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Maximum Predicted Heart Rate (MPHR):** $\text{MPHR} = 220 - \text{Age (years)}$ — Source: Astrand formula
> - **Target Heart Rate:** 85% of MPHR (ranges from 70%–85% depending on clinical goal)
> - **Metabolic Equivalents (METs):** 1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂ / kg / min
> - **Standard Paper Speed:** 25 mm/s ; **Standard Calibration:** 10 mm = 1 mV

---

## 1.2 Arrhythmia Monitors

### Formal Academic Definition
An **Arrhythmia Monitor** is a biomedical electronic device designed for the continuous, real-time detection, classification, and recording of abnormal cardiac rhythms (**arrhythmias**) from the surface ECG. Modern arrhythmia monitors employ **microprocessor-based digital signal processing (DSP)** algorithms — most notably the **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm** (1985) — to perform QRS complex detection, R-R interval measurement, and rhythm classification. The system must comply with the **ANSI/AAMI EC13** standard for cardiac monitors, which mandates specific alarm and detection performance criteria.

### Types of Arrhythmia Monitors
- **Bedside / ICU Arrhythmia Monitor:** Used in coronary care units; continuous 3-lead or 5-lead ECG monitoring with on-line arrhythmia detection and alarm.
- **Holter Monitor (Ambulatory ECG):** A portable, battery-operated, digital recorder worn by the patient for **24 to 48 hours** (sometimes up to 7 days); ECG is stored in solid-state memory and analyzed post-hoc.
- **Event Monitor / Loop Recorder:** Records only when activated by the patient during symptoms, or automatically upon detecting a pre-defined arrhythmia.
- **Implantable Loop Recorder (ILR):** Subcutaneously implanted device for long-term (up to 3 years) monitoring of unexplained syncope.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a security guard watching a long video feed of a busy street. The guard is trained to recognize "normal" pedestrian movement and instantly alert when something unusual happens — a person falling, running erratically, or standing still too long. An arrhythmia monitor does the same for the heart: it learns the "normal" pattern of QRS complexes, measures the precise **R-R intervals** between beats, and flags any deviation — a missed beat (**PVC**, **PAC**), a run of fast beats (**VT**, **VF**), or a dangerously slow rhythm (**Bradycardia**, **Asystole**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Reference:**
> The minimum **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** required for a diagnostic-quality ECG amplifier used in both stress testing and arrhythmia monitoring is **≥ 100 dB** (AAMI EC11 standard), and the bandwidth is typically **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** for diagnostic ECG, narrowing to **0.5 Hz to 40 Hz** for monitoring-only applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Cardiac Stress Test System

A cardiac stress test system is a **multi-modal, real-time patient monitoring platform** with built-in safety interlocks. Its operational logic can be broken down into the following structured steps:

1. **Patient Preparation & Electrode Placement**
   The patient's skin is cleaned with alcohol and lightly abraded. **10 electrodes** (for a standard 12-lead ECG) are placed on the limbs and chest in accordance with the Mason-Likar modification (limb electrodes moved to the torso to minimize exercise artifact).

2. **Baseline Recording (Pre-Exercise Phase)**
   A **supine** and **standing** baseline ECG and blood pressure are recorded. This allows comparison with the stress phase and helps identify resting abnormalities.

3. **Application of Controlled Physical Stress**
   The patient walks on a **motorized treadmill** (or pedals a cycle ergometer) whose speed and grade follow a defined protocol. The **Bruce Protocol** is the most widely used; it has **7 stages** of **3 minutes each**, with progressive increase in speed (1.7 mph → 6.0 mph) and grade (10% → 22%).

4. **Continuous Multi-Parameter Monitoring**
   - **ECG (12 leads):** Continuously displayed, with **ST-segment trending** in real time.
   - **Heart Rate:** Computed beat-to-beat from the R-R interval.
   - **Blood Pressure:** Manually with sphygmomanometer or automatically with an automated cuff every 2–3 minutes.
   - **Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂):** Optional, via finger pulse oximeter.
   - **Symptoms:** Patient-reported chest pain, dyspnea, dizziness.

5. **End-Point Determination**
   The test is terminated when the patient reaches the **target heart rate (85% MPHR)**, develops **significant ST depression (≥ 2 mm)**, experiences **chest pain**, shows **dangerous arrhythmia**, or shows a **drop in systolic BP > 20 mmHg**.

6. **Recovery Phase**
   Exercise is stopped, but monitoring continues for **6 to 10 minutes** until the ECG, HR, and BP return to near-baseline values. Ischemic changes during recovery are particularly diagnostic of CAD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight — Why the Bruce Protocol Works:**
> The Bruce Protocol increases the workload (and therefore oxygen demand) in **discrete MET-level steps**. Each stage corresponds to an approximately **2–3 MET increase**, pushing the patient from ~5 METs (Stage 1) to ~13 METs (Stage 7). This graded stress uncovers ischemia that is rate-dependent.

---

## 2.2 Operating Principle of an Arrhythmia Monitor

The arrhythmia monitor operates on the principle of **continuous QRS detection + R-R interval analysis + rhythm classification**. The key steps are:

1. **Signal Acquisition** — Surface ECG electrodes (typically a 5-lead configuration) capture the bioelectric signal of the heart.
2. **Pre-amplification** — A high-input-impedance, high-CMRR instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128) boosts the ~1 mV ECG signal.
3. **Bandpass Filtering** — A 0.5–40 Hz bandpass filter removes baseline wander and high-frequency noise (electromyogram, line interference).
4. **Notch Filtering** — A 50 Hz / 60 Hz notch filter removes mains interference.
5. **QRS Detection** — The Pan-Tompkins algorithm applies: **bandpass filter (5–15 Hz) → derivative → squaring → moving-window integration → adaptive thresholding → decision rule**.
6. **R-R Interval Computation** — Time between consecutive R-peaks.
7. **Arrhythmia Classification** — Logic based on R-R interval thresholds:
   - **Bradycardia:** HR < 50 bpm
   - **Tachycardia:** HR > 100 bpm
   - **Asystole:** No QRS for > 6 seconds
   - **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF):** Irregular, no organized QRS, HR equivalent > 300 bpm
   - **Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC):** QRS width > 120 ms, no preceding P-wave, followed by compensatory pause
8. **Alarm Generation & Storage** — Audible/visual alarms; ECG segment stored to memory.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | Instantaneous heart rate from R-R interval | bpm ; $T_{RR}$ in seconds |
| $\text{HR} = \dfrac{1500}{N}$ | Heart rate from ECG paper (at 25 mm/s) | bpm ; $N$ = small-boxes between R-peaks (1 box = 1 mm = 0.04 s) |
| $\text{MPHR} = 220 - \text{Age}$ | Maximum Predicted Heart Rate (Astrand) | bpm ; valid for adults |
| $\text{THR} = 0.85 \times \text{MPHR}$ | Target Heart Rate (85% of MPHR) | bpm |
| $\text{METs} = \dfrac{\dot{V}O_2}{3.5}$ | Metabolic Equivalent of Task | dimensionless ; $\dot{V}O_2$ in mL/kg/min |
| $\dot{V}O_2 \approx 3.5 + 0.17 \times v + 0.09 \times v \cdot g$ | ACSM treadmill equation (approx) | mL/kg/min ; $v$ = speed in m/min, $g$ = grade in % |
| $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | dB ; $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain |
| $\text{THR}_{\text{lower}} = 0.70 \times \text{MPHR}$ | Lower training limit (70% MPHR) | bpm |
| $\text{THR}_{\text{upper}} = 0.85 \times \text{MPHR}$ | Upper training limit (85% MPHR) | bpm |
| $\Delta \text{ST} \geq 1 \text{ mm} (0.1 \text{ mV})$ | Positive test threshold (horizontal/down-sloping) | measured 80 ms post J-point |
| $T_{\text{recovery}} \geq 6 \text{ min}$ | Minimum recovery monitoring time | minutes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Markdown Note:** In the table above, all absolute-value and division operators have been written using `\dfrac` or `\vert` syntax to ensure no vertical pipes break the markdown table rendering.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Stress Test Systems** are used in sports medicine, pre-operative cardiac risk assessment, and post-MI rehabilitation planning.
- **Arrhythmia Monitors** form the backbone of every ICU, CCU, and telemetry ward, and are mandated by the **AAMI EC13** standard for alarm priority, accuracy of heart rate meter, and alarm silence/reset behavior.
- The **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm** has become the de facto standard for real-time QRS detection and is implemented in nearly every commercial ECG/arrhythmia product.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heart Rate from R-R Interval

### Setup
The ECG paper moves at a standard speed of $v_p = 25 \text{ mm/s}$. On the standard ECG paper, 1 small box $= 1 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ s}$, and 1 large box $= 5 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ s}$.

### Step 1 — Relate R-R distance on paper to time
If the distance between two consecutive R-peaks on the paper is $d_{RR}$ millimeters, then the time interval $T_{RR}$ between the two beats is given by:

$$
T_{RR} = \dfrac{d_{RR}}{v_p}
$$

Substituting the standard paper speed $v_p = 25 \text{ mm/s}$:

$$
T_{RR} = \dfrac{d_{RR}}{25} \quad \text{(seconds)}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert R-R interval to heart rate
Heart rate (in beats per minute, **bpm**) is the reciprocal of the time between consecutive beats, multiplied by 60 (seconds per minute):

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $T_{RR}$
Replacing $T_{RR}$ from Step 1:

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{\,d_{RR}/25\,} = \dfrac{60 \times 25}{d_{RR}} = \dfrac{1500}{d_{RR}}
$$

### Step 4 — Express in terms of number of small boxes
Since 1 small box $= 1 \text{ mm}$, the distance in millimeters $d_{RR}$ equals the number of small boxes $N$ between the R-peaks:

$$
\boxed{\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{1500}{N}}
$$

> This is the **"1500 method"** — the most frequently used formula in KTU clinical examination problems.

### Step 5 — Numerical Verification
Suppose $N = 20$ small boxes between R-peaks. Then:

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{1500}{20} = 75 \text{ bpm}
$$

This corresponds to a healthy resting adult.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Predicted Heart Rate and Target Heart Rate

### Step 1 — Astrand's Age-Predicted Maximum HR Formula
Based on population studies, the maximum heart rate achievable during exercise decreases approximately linearly with age. Astrand proposed:

$$
\text{MPHR} = 220 - \text{Age (years)}
$$

### Step 2 — Target Heart Rate
The target heart rate is the range within which the heart is exercising aerobically and is most informative diagnostically. The standard range is 70%–85% of MPHR:

$$
\text{THR}_{\text{lower}} = 0.70 \times (220 - \text{Age})
$$

$$
\text{THR}_{\text{upper}} = 0.85 \times (220 - \text{Age})
$$

### Step 3 — Worked Example
A 50-year-old patient undergoes a Bruce Protocol stress test. Calculate the maximum predicted heart rate and the target heart rate range.

**MPHR calculation:**

$$
\text{MPHR} = 220 - 50 = 170 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Target HR range:**

$$
\text{THR}_{\text{lower}} = 0.70 \times 170 = 119 \text{ bpm}
$$

$$
\text{THR}_{\text{upper}} = 0.85 \times 170 = 144.5 \approx 145 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The patient should achieve between **119 and 145 bpm** for a diagnostically valid test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Treadmill Workload and METs (ACSM Treadmill Equation)

### Step 1 — Workload on Treadmill
The horizontal work rate (in kgm/min) and vertical work rate (in kgm/min) for a treadmill walking exercise are:

$$
W_{\text{horizontal}} = \text{body weight (kg)} \times \text{speed (m/min)}
$$

$$
W_{\text{vertical}} = \text{body weight (kg)} \times \text{speed (m/min)} \times \text{grade (decimal)}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Oxygen Consumption (ACSM, 1975)
The classic ACSM equation for estimating steady-state oxygen consumption on a treadmill is:

$$
\dot{V}O_2 = 3.5 + 0.17 \times v + 0.09 \times v \cdot g \quad \text{(mL/kg/min)}
$$

where $v$ is treadmill speed in **m/min** and $g$ is grade in **%**.

### Step 3 — Convert to METs

$$
\text{METs} = \dfrac{\dot{V}O_2}{3.5}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example (Bruce Protocol Stage 1)
A 70-kg patient at Bruce Stage 1: speed = 1.7 mph, grade = 10%.

**Step (a) — Convert speed to m/min:**

$$
v = 1.7 \text{ mph} \times 26.8 \text{ (m/min per mph)} = 45.56 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step (b) — Compute oxygen consumption:**

$$
\dot{V}O_2 = 3.5 + 0.17 \times 45.56 + 0.09 \times 45.56 \times 0.10
$$

$$
\dot{V}O_2 = 3.5 + 7.7452 + 0.41 = 11.66 \text{ mL/kg/min}
$$

**Step (c) — Convert to METs:**

$$
\text{METs} = \dfrac{11.66}{3.5} \approx 3.33 \text{ METs}
$$

This matches the expected MET level of Bruce Stage 1 (~4.8 METs in published tables; our approximation yields ~3.3 METs, which is a rough estimate — published METs include body-mass correction).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: QRS Detection Thresholds (Pan-Tompkins Class)

| **Rhythm Class** | **R-R Criterion** | **QRS Width** | **P-wave** | **Clinical Alarm** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Normal Sinus Rhythm (NSR) | 0.6 s $\leq T_{RR} \leq$ 1.0 s | < 120 ms | Present, regular | None |
| Sinus Bradycardia | $T_{RR} > 1.2$ s (HR < 50) | < 120 ms | Present | Yellow / Advisory |
| Sinus Tachycardia | $T_{RR} < 0.6$ s (HR > 100) | < 120 ms | Present | Yellow / Advisory |
| Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC) | Premature + compensatory pause | > 120 ms | Absent | Yellow |
| Ventricular Tachycardia (VT) | $\geq 3$ wide QRS in a row, HR > 100 | > 120 ms | Absent | Red (Lethal) |
| Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) | Disorganized, no QRS, HR equiv. > 300 | n/a | n/a | Red (Lethal) |
| Asystole | No QRS for $\geq 6$ s | n/a | n/a | Red (Lethal) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Cardiac Stress Test (Treadmill) System

The following Mermaid diagram represents the complete **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a modern stress test system, mapping the analog signal chain, the safety subsystems, and the digital processing chain.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PAT[Patient on Treadmill] --> TM[Motorized Treadmill with Bruce Protocol Controller]
    TM --> EX[Exercise Stress Workload in METs]

    ELEC[Ten ECG Electrodes Mason-Likar Configuration] --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620 Gain 1000]
    ELEC --> BP[Automated Blood Pressure Cuff]
    ELEC --> SPO2[SpO2 Pulse Oximeter]

    IA --> BPF[Bandpass Filter 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz]
    BPF --> NOTCH[Notch Filter 50 Hz or 60 Hz]
    NOTCH --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 12 bit 1 kHz]

    ADC --> CPU[Microprocessor with Pan-Tompkins DSP]
    CPU --> STR[ST Segment Trend Analyzer]
    CPU --> ARR[Arrhythmia Detector]
    CPU --> HRD[Heart Rate Display bpm]
    CPU --> MET[MET Level Display]
    CPU --> REC[Digital Storage 12 lead]

    STR --> MON[Color Monitor with 12 lead Live ECG]
    ARR --> MON
    HRD --> MON
    MET --> MON

    ARR --> ALM[Audible and Visual Alarms 3 priority levels]
    STR --> ALM

    CPU --> PRN[Thermal Array Printer 12 lead Report]
    REC --> PRN

    CPU --> ESW[Emergency Stop Switch]
    ESW --> TM

    ESW --> DEF[Biphasic Defibrillator Standby on Cart]
    CPU --> OXY[Emergency Oxygen Supply]

    subgraph SafetySubsystem
        ESW
        DEF
        OXY
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Block Explanation:**
> - **IA (Instrumentation Amplifier):** Provides the high CMRR ($\geq 100$ dB) needed to reject common-mode 50/60 Hz interference and motion artifact.
> - **BPF + NOTCH:** Cascaded to clean the ECG of baseline wander and mains noise — essential because stress testing amplifies motion artifact.
> - **Pan-Tompkins DSP block:** Runs in real-time on the microprocessor; detects each QRS, computes R-R, and feeds the heart-rate display.
> - **ST Trend Analyzer:** The most clinically critical block — continuously tracks the J-point + 80 ms ST amplitude and trends it across all 12 leads.
> - **Safety Subsystem (red block):** Mandated by hospital practice; the operator can halt the treadmill instantly, and a defibrillator must be within arm's reach.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Arrhythmia Monitor (Bedside / Holter)

The arrhythmia monitor architecture is a streamlined version of the stress-test chain, optimized for **continuous QRS detection and rhythm classification** over hours to days.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PAT[Patient] --> ELEC2[Three to Five Lead ECG Electrodes]
    ELEC2 --> IA2[Instrumentation Amplifier High CMRR 100 dB min]
    ELEC2 --> RT[Right Leg Drive Circuit Driven Ground]

    IA2 --> HPF[High Pass Filter 0.5 Hz removes baseline wander]
    HPF --> LPF[Low Pass Filter 40 Hz removes EMG noise]
    LPF --> NF2[Notch Filter 50 Hz or 60 Hz]
    NF2 --> ADC2[Analog to Digital Converter 12 to 16 bit 500 Hz]

    ADC2 --> PT[Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection]
    PT --> RR[R to R Interval Counter in ms]
    RR --> CLS[Rhythm Classification Engine]

    CLS --> NSR[Normal Sinus Rhythm]
    CLS --> BRA[Bradycardia HR less than 50]
    CLS --> TAC[Tachycardia HR greater than 100]
    CLS --> PVC[Premature Ventricular Contraction]
    CLS --> VT[Ventricular Tachycardia 3 wide beats]
    CLS --> VF[Ventricular Fibrillation]
    CLS --> ASY[Asystole 6 s no QRS]

    PT --> MEM[Non volatile Memory 24 to 48 h Holter]
    PT --> DSP2[Display 2 to 3 lead real time ECG]
    PT --> ALM2[Alarm Generator AAMI EC13 priority]

    MEM --> PC[Offline Analysis PC with full disclosure]

    subgraph ArrhythmiaAlarmSubsystem
        BRA
        TAC
        PVC
        VT
        VF
        ASY
        ALM2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Notes:**
> - The **Right-Leg Drive (RLD) circuit** actively drives the patient's right leg with an inverted common-mode signal, boosting the practical CMRR by another 20–40 dB.
> - The **Bandwidth choice (0.5–40 Hz)** follows the **AAMI EC13** recommendation for monitoring ECG — narrower than the diagnostic 0.05–150 Hz band, because the goal here is rhythm (not morphology).
> - **Pan-Tompkins block** typically achieves **> 99% sensitivity** and **> 99.5% positive predictivity** on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database — the gold standard reference.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define Cardiac Stress Test. List any three indications for performing a stress test.**

**Model Answer:**
A cardiac stress test is a non-invasive diagnostic procedure in which the patient performs graded physical exercise (typically on a treadmill or cycle ergometer) under continuous ECG, blood pressure, and heart-rate monitoring, with the objective of detecting myocardial ischemia, evaluating functional capacity, and assessing exercise-induced arrhythmias.

**Three indications (any three, 1 mark each):**
1. Diagnosis of suspected Coronary Artery Disease (CAD) in patients with chest pain.
2. Assessment of functional capacity following myocardial infarction or revascularization.
3. Evaluation of exercise-induced arrhythmias.
4. Pre-operative cardiac risk assessment before major non-cardiac surgery.
5. Evaluation of response to anti-anginal or anti-arrhythmic therapy.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is CMRR? Why is high CMRR important in an ECG amplifier used for stress testing?**

**Model Answer:**
**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is the ability of a differential amplifier to reject signals common to both inputs, defined as:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)
$$

**Importance in stress testing:**
During stress testing, the patient is moving vigorously, producing large **50/60 Hz electromagnetic interference** and **motion artifact** that appear as common-mode voltages to the ECG amplifier. A **high CMRR ($\geq$ 100 dB, as per AAMI EC11)** is essential to:
- Reject mains interference coupled to the body.
- Reject motion-induced common-mode artifacts.
- Preserve the integrity of the low-amplitude ($\sim$ 1 mV) ECG signal during exercise.

---
