## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **cardiac stress test (treadmill test) system**. Explain the role of the **ST-segment trend analyzer** and the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** within the system. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **55-year-old patient** undergoes a Bruce Protocol stress test. The R-R interval on the ECG monitor is measured to be **0.50 seconds** during peak exercise. Calculate the **(i)** Heart Rate, **(ii)** Maximum Predicted HR, and **(iii)** Target HR range. Comment on whether the patient has reached the target. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 for the detailed Mermaid block diagram. The student should redraw a labeled version of that diagram in ink. Key blocks to be drawn and labeled:)

Patient $\rightarrow$ ECG Electrodes (Mason-Likar) $\rightarrow$ Instrumentation Amplifier $\rightarrow$ 0.05–150 Hz Bandpass Filter $\rightarrow$ 50/60 Hz Notch Filter $\rightarrow$ A/D Converter $\rightarrow$ Microprocessor (running Pan-Tompkins + ST analyzer) $\rightarrow$ Display + Printer + Alarm + Emergency Subsystem.

**Working description (key points):**
1. Patient is connected to a 12-lead ECG using **Mason-Likar electrode placement**; BP cuff and SpO₂ sensor are also attached.
2. The patient walks on a **motorized treadmill** whose speed and grade follow the **Bruce Protocol** (7 stages, 3 min each).
3. The **ECG signal** is amplified (gain $\approx$ 1000) by an instrumentation amplifier with CMRR $\geq$ 100 dB, bandpass filtered, and digitized.
4. The **microprocessor** runs the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for QRS detection: 5–15 Hz bandpass $\rightarrow$ derivative $\rightarrow$ squaring $\rightarrow$ moving-window integration $\rightarrow$ adaptive threshold.
5. The **ST-segment trend analyzer** continuously measures the **ST amplitude at 80 ms after the J-point** in all 12 leads and trends it over time. A horizontal/down-sloping ST depression of **$\geq$ 1 mm** indicates myocardial ischemia.
6. Heart rate (bpm) and MET level are computed and displayed in real time; **3-priority alarms** (advisory / yellow / red) are raised per AAMI EC13.
7. **Safety subsystem**: emergency stop button, defibrillator on standby, oxygen supply — all mandatory per hospital protocol.

**Role of ST-segment trend analyzer (key points):**
- Tracks the J-point + 80 ms amplitude across all 12 leads.
- Plots ST level (mm) versus time — a temporal trend.
- Detects **ischemic ST depression** $\geq$ 1 mm (positive test).
- Compares baseline ST with peak-exercise and recovery ST.
- Provides the most diagnostically valuable output of the entire system.

**Role of Pan-Tompkins algorithm (key points):**
- Real-time, beat-by-beat QRS detection.
- Enables accurate R-R interval measurement and hence HR computation.
- Feeds the **arrhythmia detector** with beat markers and widths.
- Sensitivity > 99% on MIT-BIH database.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** Age = 55 years ; R-R interval at peak exercise $T_{RR} = 0.50$ s

**(i) Heart Rate:**

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}} = \dfrac{60}{0.50} = 120 \text{ bpm}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Predicted HR (Astrand):**

$$
\text{MPHR} = 220 - \text{Age} = 220 - 55 = 165 \text{ bpm}
$$

**(iii) Target HR range (70% – 85% of MPHR):**

$$
\text{THR}_{\text{lower}} = 0.70 \times 165 = 115.5 \text{ bpm}
$$

$$
\text{THR}_{\text{upper}} = 0.85 \times 165 = 140.25 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Target HR range = 115.5 to 140.25 bpm**

**Comment:** The patient's peak HR of **120 bpm** lies **within the target range (115.5 – 140.25 bpm)**. Therefore, the patient has **achieved the target heart rate**, and the stress test is **diagnostically valid**. The percentage of MPHR achieved is:

$$
\% \text{MPHR} = \dfrac{120}{165} \times 100\% \approx 72.7\%
$$

This is within the acceptable sub-maximal exercise range (≥ 85% of MPHR is preferred, but 72.7% is acceptable for clinical interpretation, especially in patients on beta-blockers).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **bedside arrhythmia monitor**. List the major classes of arrhythmias the monitor can detect. (7 marks)

**(b)** An ECG shows **30 small boxes** between two consecutive R-peaks at a standard paper speed of **25 mm/s**. Calculate the **(i)** Heart Rate, and **(ii)** the R-R interval in seconds. The patient is a **45-year-old male**. Compute the **MPHR** and state whether the patient is in **bradycardia, normal sinus rhythm, or tachycardia**. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 for the Mermaid block diagram. Student should draw a clean labeled version.)

Patient $\rightarrow$ 3–5 lead ECG electrodes $\rightarrow$ Instrumentation Amplifier (with RLD) $\rightarrow$ 0.5–40 Hz bandpass $\rightarrow$ Notch filter $\rightarrow$ ADC $\rightarrow$ Pan-Tompkins QRS detector $\rightarrow$ R-R interval counter $\rightarrow$ Rhythm classifier $\rightarrow$ Display + Memory + Alarms.

**Working description (key points, 1 mark each):**
1. ECG electrodes (3–5 leads) pick up cardiac electrical activity.
2. Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620) provides **differential gain of $\sim$ 1000** and **CMRR $\geq$ 100 dB**; the **Right-Leg Drive (RLD) circuit** further boosts practical CMRR.
3. **Bandpass filter (0.5–40 Hz)** removes baseline wander (DC) and EMG / high-frequency noise — this is the AAMI EC13 monitoring bandwidth.
4. **Notch filter** removes 50/60 Hz mains interference.
5. **A/D converter** digitizes the ECG at 250–500 Hz with 12–16 bit resolution.
6. **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** runs in real-time on the microprocessor for QRS detection.
7. **R-R interval counter** computes the time between consecutive R-peaks.
8. **Rhythm classifier** applies threshold-based rules to identify arrhythmias.
9. Detected arrhythmias trigger **audible/visual alarms** per AAMI EC13 priority (high / medium / low).
10. ECG is stored continuously in memory and displayed in real time.

**Major classes of arrhythmias detected (list any 4–5, 1 mark):**
- Normal Sinus Rhythm (NSR)
- Sinus Bradycardia
- Sinus Tachycardia
- Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC)
- Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)
- Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)
- Asystole
- Atrial Fibrillation
- Premature Atrial Contraction (PAC)

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $N = 30$ small boxes between R-peaks ; paper speed = 25 mm/s ; Age = 45 years

**(i) Heart Rate (1500 method):**

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{1500}{N} = \dfrac{1500}{30} = 50 \text{ bpm}
$$

**(ii) R-R interval in seconds:**

Since 1 small box = 0.04 s:

$$
T_{RR} = N \times 0.04 = 30 \times 0.04 = 1.20 \text{ s}
$$

**Verification using the 60 method:**

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}} = \dfrac{60}{1.20} = 50 \text{ bpm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Maximum Predicted HR (Astrand):**

$$
\text{MPHR} = 220 - 45 = 175 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Rhythm classification:**

| **Criterion** | **Threshold** | **Observed** | **Classification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| HR | 50 bpm | < 60 bpm | **Bradycardia** |
| QRS width | assumed normal < 120 ms | — | Likely Sinus Bradycardia |

**Conclusion:** The patient exhibits **Sinus Bradycardia (HR = 50 bpm)**, defined as HR below 60 bpm originating from the SA node. If the patient is asymptomatic and an athlete, this may be physiological. If symptomatic (dizziness, syncope), it may require further evaluation for sick sinus syndrome or AV block.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the two HR formulas** — Using `1500/N` for *monitoring* paper (valid) but applying it to a 10-second strip count (use 10 × number of R-peaks for that case). The 1500 method assumes **paper speed of 25 mm/s**; if the speed is 50 mm/s, use **3000/N** instead.
> 2. **MPHR formula misapplication** — Some students use $200 - 0.67 \times \text{Age}$ (an alternative Tanaka formula). Both are accepted, but mixing them within the same answer is penalized.
> 3. **Omitting the safety subsystem** in the stress-test block diagram. The **defibrillator, emergency stop, and oxygen supply** are **mandatory blocks** per KTU marking scheme — failure to draw them costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** in the arrhythmia-monitor block diagram. The RLD is a **distinguishing feature** of a high-quality ECG amplifier and is frequently asked.
> 5. **ST-trend analysis** is asked in nearly every KTU stress-test question. Students often draw the block diagram but fail to **describe what the ST trend analyzer actually does** (i.e., measure at J+80 ms, plot vs time, threshold $\geq$ 1 mm depression).
> 6. **Missing units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit in calculation sub-parts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cardiac Stress Test** = controlled exercise + continuous ECG/BP/HR monitoring; gold standard for diagnosing exercise-induced ischemia.
- **Bruce Protocol** = 7 stages, 3 min each, graded treadmill test; most widely used.
- **Positive test criterion** = **$\geq$ 1 mm horizontal or down-sloping ST depression at 80 ms after the J-point**.
- **End-point criteria** = target HR reached, $\geq$ 2 mm ST depression, chest pain, dangerous arrhythmia, BP drop, or patient exhaustion.
- **Recovery monitoring** must continue for **at least 6 minutes**.
- **MPHR (Astrand)** = $220 - \text{Age}$ ; **THR** = 70%–85% of MPHR.
- **Heart Rate formulas** (at 25 mm/s): $\text{HR} = 1500/N$ or $\text{HR} = 60/T_{RR}$.
- **Arrhythmia Monitor** = continuous QRS detection + R-R analysis + rhythm classification.
- **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm** = bandpass (5–15 Hz) $\rightarrow$ derivative $\rightarrow$ squaring $\rightarrow$ integration $\rightarrow$ adaptive threshold.
- **CMRR** must be **$\geq$ 100 dB** (AAMI EC11) for diagnostic ECG; ECG amplifier gain $\approx$ 1000.
- **Diagnostic bandwidth** = 0.05–150 Hz ; **Monitoring bandwidth** = 0.5–40 Hz.
- **AAMI EC13** standard governs alarm priority, HR meter accuracy, and arrhythmia monitor behavior.
- **Asystole** = no QRS for $\geq$ 6 s ; **VF** = disorganized, no QRS, equivalent rate > 300 bpm.
- **Block diagram must include** safety subsystem (defibrillator, emergency stop, oxygen) — KTU marks awarded for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambulatory Recorders and Holter Monitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT504 - ANALYTICAL AND DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ambulatory Recorders and Holter Monitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ambulatory Monitoring** refers to the continuous, portable, and non-invasive recording of physiological signals from a freely-moving patient outside the confines of a clinical or hospital environment, typically over an extended duration (ranging from **24 hours** to several days, weeks, or even years in advanced implantable systems).

A **Holter Monitor** (also called an *ambulatory electrocardiography device* or *ambulatory ECG recorder*) is a portable, battery-operated biomedical instrument designed for the continuous recording of cardiac electrical activity (electrocardiogram) for a minimum of **24 hours** during a patient's normal daily routine, including sleep, exercise, and stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Distinction:** A *Holter Monitor* specifically refers to **continuous ECG recording devices**, whereas *Ambulatory Recorders* is a broader category that includes ECG Holters, ambulatory blood pressure monitors (ABPM), EEG ambulatory systems, and event/loop recorders. In KTU question papers, expect explicit differentiation between these terms.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **security CCTV camera** for the heart.

| Clinical Scenario | Real-World Analogy |
|------------------|--------------------|
| Patient in hospital bed with bedside ECG monitor | A stationary security camera watching a single shop door for a few hours |
| Holter Monitor attached to a free-moving patient | A body-camera worn by a police officer for 24 hours during their full patrol — capturing every incident, including the rare ones that happen on the road |
| Event/Loop Recorder (implantable) | A car's dashcam set to "parking mode" — only saves the 5 minutes before and after a sudden bump (cardiac event) |

### The Core Problem Holter Monitors Solve:
- A standard **12-lead ECG** captures only **~10 seconds** of cardiac activity. Many arrhythmias (irregular heartbeats) — like **paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, intermittent ST-segment changes, or silent ischemia** — occur sporadically, sometimes only once every few hours, and almost never happen during the brief in-clinic ECG.
- Therefore, a **prolonged recording window** is mathematically necessary to catch these "needle-in-a-haystack" events.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Intuition for the Student:** The probability of detecting a rare cardiac event is **directly proportional to the monitoring duration**. If an arrhythmia occurs once every 6 hours, a 10-second ECG has roughly a **0.05% chance** of catching it, while a 24-hour Holter has nearly a **100% chance**.

---

## 1.3 Historical Context & Key Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Inventor:** Norman J. Holter (biophysicist), who pioneered the concept in **1957** with a **75-pound backpack-mounted transmitter** that wirelessly broadcast ECG to a receiver.
> - **Modern Mass:** Current Holter recorders weigh approximately **<100 grams** (often just **30–50 g** for patch devices).
> - **Standard Recording Duration:** **24 hours** (clinical gold standard) to **48–72 hours** (extended Holter), up to **14 days** (patch ECG), and **up to 3 years** (implantable loop recorders).
> - **Sampling Frequency:** Modern digital Holters record at **125 Hz to 1000 Hz** per channel, with ADC resolutions of **10–16 bits**.
> - **Frequency Response:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (per AHA recommendations for ambulatory ECG).
> - **Storage Capacity:** Modern solid-state Holter recorders use flash memory from **256 MB to 4 GB**, storing 24–48 hours of 3-channel data.

---

## 1.4 Standard KTU Classification of Ambulatory Recorders

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with this taxonomy:

1. **Continuous Holter Recorders** (24–72 hour full-disclosure recorders).
2. **Event Recorders** (patient-activated upon symptom onset).
3. **Loop Recorders** (continuous, but only retain a sliding window around an event).
4. **Implantable Loop Recorders (ILR)** — *e.g., Medtronic Reveal LINQ*.
5. **Patch ECG Monitors** — *e.g., iRhythm Zio, VitalConnect*.
6. **Mobile Cardiac Telemetry (MCT)** — real-time wireless transmission to a monitoring center.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — "The Why and How"

### Step 1: Bioelectric Signal Acquisition
The heart's depolarization and repolarization generate ionic currents that propagate through body tissues. Surface electrodes (typically **Ag/AgCl** pre-gelled disposable electrodes) placed on the chest detect these potentials.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Theory Point:** The skin-electrode interface acts as a **half-cell potential** source combined with a **polarization impedance** that must be minimized via skin preparation (alcohol cleaning, mild abrasion) to keep the **electrode-skin impedance below 5 kΩ** at 10 Hz.

The detected signal amplitude is typically:

$$V_{ECG} \approx 0.5 \text{ mV to 5 mV (peak-to-peak)}$$

### Step 2: Front-End Amplification
A Holter recorder uses an **instrumentation amplifier** with the following key specs:
- **Differential gain (A):** Typically **1000 V/V** (60 dB).
- **CMRR:** > **80 dB** at 50/60 Hz to reject powerline interference.
- **Input impedance:** > **10 MΩ** to avoid loading the high-impedance skin electrodes.
- **Noise:** < **5 µV RMS** (referred to input) — critical because the ECG signal itself is in the millivolt range, so noise must be sub-millivolt.

The amplified signal is:

$$V_{out} = A \cdot (V_{lead+} - V_{lead-})$$

where $A$ is the differential gain of the instrumentation amplifier.

### Step 3: Bandpass Filtering
To isolate the QRS complex and reject baseline wander, motion artifact, and high-frequency noise, a **bandpass filter** is applied:

$$H(s) = \frac{s \cdot \omega_{H}}{s + \omega_{H}} \cdot \frac{\omega_{L}}{s + \omega_{L}}$$

where:
- $\omega_{L} = 2\pi f_{L}$ → high-pass corner (typically **0.05 Hz**)
- $\omega_{H} = 2\pi f_{H}$ → low-pass corner (typically **150 Hz**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Point:** Why is the lower cutoff 0.05 Hz and not DC (0 Hz)?
> **Answer:** A pure DC-coupled amplifier would saturate from the electrode **half-cell potential** (offset of ~200–300 mV), and from baseline wander caused by respiration (typically 0.1–0.5 Hz). A 0.05 Hz high-pass filter removes these slow drifts while preserving the ECG ST-segment (which carries diagnostic information about ischemia).

### Step 4: Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
- **Resolution:** 10–16 bits.
- **Sampling rate:** $f_s \geq 250$ Hz (Nyquist requirement: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ where $f_{max} \approx 125$ Hz for adult ECG).
- **Quantization step size:**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale voltage range and $n$ is the number of bits.

> **Example (KTU Calculation Style):**
> For a 12-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 5$ V:
> $$\Delta = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} \approx 1.22 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

### Step 5: Storage and/or Transmission
- **Solid-state storage** (SD card / flash memory) for traditional Holter.
- **Bluetooth / cellular transmission** for Mobile Cardiac Telemetry (MCT) systems.

### Step 6: Offline/Online Analysis
- **QRS detection** (Pan-Tompkins algorithm is the gold standard).
- **Heart rate variability (HRV)** analysis.
- **ST-segment and T-wave morphology analysis** for ischemia detection.
- **Arrhythmia classification** (e.g., Lown classification for PVCs).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Differential Amplifier Output | $V_{out} = A_d (V_{+} - V_{-})$ | $A_d$ = differential gain (V/V) |
| 2 | CMRR | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | in dB |
| 3 | ECG Amplitude Range | $V_{ECG} = 0.5 \text{ to } 5 \text{ mV}$ | millivolts peak-to-peak |
| 4 | Bandpass Filter | $H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega/\omega_L}{(1 + j\omega/\omega_L)(1 + j\omega/\omega_H)}$ | $\omega_L, \omega_H$ in rad/s |
| 5 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 6 | ADC Quantization Step | $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^n$ | $n$ = bits |
| 7 | Storage Requirement (approx) | $M = n_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot n \cdot T \cdot 3600$ | bytes; $T$ in hours |
| 8 | Heart Rate from R-R | $HR = \frac{60}{RR}$ | RR in seconds, HR in bpm |
| 9 | Beat Detection Sensitivity (statistical) | $P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | $p$ = per-sample event prob., $n$ = number of beats |
| 10 | Battery Life | $t_{batt} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{load}}$ | $C$ in mAh, $I$ in mA |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **bandpass filter formula (#4)**, the **Nyquist criterion (#5)**, and the **ADC quantization step (#6)**. They appear almost every year in short-answer or derivation questions.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Cardiology** | Detection of silent ischemia, paroxysmal AF, syncope evaluation |
| **Pharma Clinical Trials** | QT-interval monitoring in drug safety studies (ICH E14 guideline) |
| **Sports Medicine** | ECG monitoring during marathons, athletic training |
| **Occupational Health** | Stress ECG of pilots, divers, high-risk workers |
| **AI/ML Research** | Massive ECG datasets (e.g., MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database) used to train deep-learning models |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Sampling Rate for Pediatric vs. Adult ECG

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A Holter monitor designer must decide the ADC sampling rate. Given that the highest meaningful ECG frequency component is $f_{max} = 125$ Hz for adult ECG and $f_{max} = 250$ Hz for pediatric ECG (because of higher heart rates and sharper QRS), apply the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem and recommend appropriate sampling rates including a 25% safety margin.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Recall the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

This is the absolute minimum sampling rate needed to perfectly reconstruct a bandlimited signal from its samples.

**Step 2: Calculate the theoretical minimum for adult ECG**

$$f_{s,adult} \geq 2 \times 125 \text{ Hz} = 250 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Add the 25% safety margin (engineering practice to account for non-ideal anti-aliasing filters)**

$$f_{s,adult,design} = 1.25 \times 250 = 312.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Round up to the nearest standard rate (250 Hz is the clinical standard)**

$$f_{s,adult,chosen} = 500 \text{ Hz} \text{ (a common standard)}$$

**Step 5: Calculate for pediatric ECG**

$$f_{s,pediatric} \geq 2 \times 250 \text{ Hz} = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6: Apply safety margin**

$$f_{s,pediatric,design} = 1.25 \times 500 = 625 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7: Round up to standard rate**

$$f_{s,pediatric,chosen} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

> **Result:** Adult Holter = 250–500 Hz; Pediatric Holter = 500–1000 Hz.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Estimating Memory Required for a 24-Hour Holter Recording

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A 3-channel Holter monitor uses a 12-bit ADC sampling at 500 Hz per channel. Calculate the total memory required to record 24 hours continuously.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Memory per sample**

$$M_{sample} = \frac{n_{bits}}{8} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5 \text{ bytes per sample}$$

**Step 2: Samples per second per channel**

$$\text{Samples/sec/channel} = 500 \text{ samples/s}$$

**Step 3: Samples per second for all 3 channels**

$$S_{total} = 3 \times 500 = 1500 \text{ samples/s}$$

**Step 4: Bytes per second**

$$B_{sec} = 1500 \times 1.5 = 2250 \text{ bytes/s}$$

**Step 5: Total seconds in 24 hours**

$$T = 24 \times 3600 = 86400 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6: Total memory**

\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= 2250 \times 86400 \\
&= 194{,}400{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 194.4 \text{ MB} \\
&\approx 185.4 \text{ MiB}
\end{aligned}

> **Result:** ~**195 MB** of storage is required. (Most modern Holters use compression algorithms that reduce this to ~30–60 MB.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of Event Detection (Statistical Basis for Holter Use)

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A patient has a paroxysmal arrhythmia that occurs once every 8 hours on average. If the probability of detection during a single 10-second in-clinic ECG is $p_1$, and during a 24-hour Holter is $p_2$, find the ratio $p_2 / p_1$. Assume the event is uniformly distributed in time.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate $p_1$**

The 10-second ECG covers $T_1 = 10$ s out of 8 hours = 28800 s.

$$p_1 = \frac{10}{28800} = 3.47 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.035\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate $p_2$**

The 24-hour Holter covers $T_2 = 24 \times 3600 = 86400$ s.

$$p_2 = \frac{86400}{28800} = 3$$

But probability cannot exceed 1, so $p_2$ is **saturated to 1 (100%)**, because 3 full event windows are covered in 24 hours.

$$p_2 = 1$$

**Step 3: Calculate the ratio**

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{1}{3.47 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2880$$

> **Result:** The Holter is approximately **2880 times** more likely to detect this arrhythmia than a single in-clinic ECG. This mathematically justifies Holter monitoring as the gold standard for paroxysmal arrhythmia detection.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CMRR Calculation

**Problem (KTU Style):**
An instrumentation amplifier has a differential gain $A_d = 1000$ and a common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.1$. Calculate the CMRR in dB.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Apply the CMRR formula**

$$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1000}{0.1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10000)$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the log**

$$CMRR = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}$$

> **Result:** CMRR = **80 dB**, which satisfies the AHA recommendation of > 80 dB for ambulatory ECG.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Parameter | Traditional Holter | Event Recorder | Loop Recorder | Patch ECG (Zio) | Implantable (ILR) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Recording Duration | 24–72 h | 30 d (battery) | 14–30 d | 14 d | Up to 3 years |
| Patient Activation | No | Yes (symptom-triggered) | Auto or manual | No | Auto or manual |
| Continuous Recording | Yes | No (only when activated) | Yes (loop buffer) | Yes | Yes (loop buffer) |
| Lead Count | 3–5 leads | 1–2 leads | 1–2 leads | 1 lead (patch) | Subcutaneous |
| Wearability | Belt/pocket | Wrist/chest | Wrist/chest | Adhesive patch | Implanted under skin |
| Data Transmission | Off-line analysis | Trans-telephonic | Trans-telephonic | USB/offline | Wireless remote |
| Best For | Frequent arrhythmias | Infrequent symptomatic events | Syncope evaluation | Long-term AF detection | Cryptogenic stroke, rare syncope |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Holter Monitor (Full Signal Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Patient Heart] --> A2[Surface Electrodes Ag/AgCl 5 to 7 leads]
    A2 --> A3[Patient Cable Shielded, driven right leg]
    A3 --> B1[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000, CMRR 80dB]
    B1 --> B2[Bandpass Filter 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz]
    B2 --> B3[Notch Filter 50 or 60 Hz rejection]
    B3 --> C1[ADC 12 to 16 bit, 250 to 1000 Hz]
    C1 --> C2[Microcontroller DSP, Pan Tompkins QRS detection]
    C2 --> D1[Flash Memory Storage 256 MB to 4 GB]
    C2 --> D2[Bluetooth Transmitter Real time MCT option]
    D1 --> E1[Offline Analysis Software]
    D2 --> E2[Cloud Monitoring Center]
    E1 --> F1[Physician Report]
    E2 --> F1
```

## 4.2 Operational Flow — Patient Wearing a Holter Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Patient visits clinic with symptoms palpitations, syncope, chest pain] --> P2[Physician orders 24 to 48 hour Holter]
    P2 --> P3[Technician attaches electrodes and recorder]
    P3 --> P4[Patient keeps activity diary]
    P4 --> P5[Patient returns after 24 to 48 hours]
    P5 --> P6[Recorder removed, data downloaded]
    P6 --> P7[Automated analysis QRS, ST, HRV]
    P7 --> P8[Technician overreads and verifies beats]
    P8 --> P9[Cardiologist interprets report]
    P9 --> P10[Diagnosis and treatment plan]
```

## 4.3 Comparison of Holter vs. Event Monitor (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Patient with suspected arrhythmia] --> Q2{Frequency of symptoms?}
    Q2 -->|Daily or more| R1[Choose 24 to 48 hour Holter]
    Q2 -->|Weekly or less| R2[Choose Event or Loop Recorder]
    Q2 -->|Very rare, cryptogenic| R3[Choose Implantable Loop Recorder]
    R1 --> S1[Captures continuous ECG, detects silent and nocturnal events]
    R2 --> S2[Patient triggered, captures symptomatic events only]
    R3 --> S3[Monitors up to 3 years, subcutaneous insertion]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Mobile Cardiac Telemetry (MCT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Heart electrical activity] --> H2[Wearable sensor 3 lead patch]
    H2 --> H3[Onboard amplifier and ADC]
    H3 --> H4[Embedded processor arrhythmia detection]
    H4 --> H5{Arrhythmia detected?}
    H5 -->|No| H6[Continue monitoring, low power mode]
    H5 -->|Yes| H7[Activate cellular transmitter]
    H7 --> H8[Cloud server receives alarm]
    H8 --> H9[Monitoring center technician 24x7]
    H9 --> H10[Physician notification if critical]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define a Holter monitor. State any two clinical indications for its use.**

**Model Answer:**
A Holter monitor is a portable, battery-operated device used for the **continuous recording of the electrocardiogram (ECG)** of an ambulatory patient for an extended period, typically **24 to 48 hours**, while the patient performs normal daily activities.

> Two clinical indications:
> 1. **Evaluation of palpitations or syncope** where a standard 12-lead ECG fails to capture the event.
> 2. **Detection of silent myocardial ischemia** by analyzing ST-segment trends over 24 hours.
> 3. *(Any other valid indication such as: post-MI risk stratification, assessment of antiarrhythmic drug efficacy, evaluation of pacemaker function.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between a Holter monitor and an event recorder.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Holter Monitor | Event Recorder |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| Recording | Continuous (24–48 h) | Only on patient activation |
| Duration | 1–2 days | Weeks to months |
| Best for | Asymptomatic / frequent events | Infrequent symptomatic events |
| Memory | Stores all data | Stores only triggered episodes |
| Data review | Full disclosure review | Episodic review |

> A Holter is the device of choice when the event is expected at least once a day; an event recorder is chosen for rarer episodes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the functional architecture of a Holter monitor. Describe the role of the instrumentation amplifier and the bandpass filter in the signal chain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-channel Holter recorder uses a 10-bit ADC with a sampling rate of 250 Hz per channel. Calculate (i) the quantization step size if the full-scale voltage is 5 V, and (ii) the total memory required to record 24 hours of continuous data.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Components:**

The Holter monitor consists of the following functional blocks (refer to **Figure 4.1** in Section 4):

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** 5 to 7 surface electrodes placed on the chest to acquire the differential biopotentials from the heart.

2. **Patient Cable (Lead Wires):** Shielded cable carrying the microvolt-level signals; includes a *driven right leg* (DRL) circuit that actively drives the body potential to reduce common-mode interference.

3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA):**
   - Provides **high differential gain** (typically 1000 V/V, or 60 dB).
   - Has very **high input impedance** (> 10 MΩ) so it does not load the high-impedance skin-electrode interface.
   - Has very **high CMRR** (> 80 dB) to reject 50/60 Hz powerline interference, which is critical because the patient is moving through environments with significant EMI.
   - Rejects the **electrode half-cell potential** of ~200–300 mV (DC offset) via AC coupling.

4. **Bandpass Filter (0.05 Hz to 150 Hz):**
   - **High-pass (0.05 Hz):** Removes baseline wander caused by respiration (0.1–0.5 Hz) and electrode DC offset.
   - **Low-pass (150 Hz):** Removes high-frequency EMG noise, motion artifact, and prevents aliasing.

5. **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz):** Rejects powerline interference; usually adaptive.

6. **ADC:** Samples the analog signal at 250–1000 Hz, 10–16 bit resolution.

7. **Microcontroller / DSP:** Runs the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for real-time QRS detection, compresses data, manages storage.

8. **Storage:** Flash memory or SD card (256 MB – 4 GB).

9. **Display / Event Marker:** Patient presses a button to mark symptomatic events.

10. **Battery:** Lithium-ion or AA, providing > 24 hours of continuous operation.

> **Role of the IA:** First-stage amplification; sets the noise floor and CMRR of the entire system.
> **Role of the Bandpass Filter:** Frequency selection; ensures only diagnostically relevant ECG components are retained.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**(i) Quantization step size:**

Given: $n = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V.

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

**(ii) Total memory for 24 hours:**

Step 1: Bytes per sample:
$$M_{sample} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25 \text{ bytes/sample}$$

Step 2: Samples per second (all 3 channels):
$$S = 3 \times 250 = 750 \text{ samples/s}$$

Step 3: Bytes per second:
$$B_{sec} = 750 \times 1.25 = 937.5 \text{ bytes/s}$$

Step 4: Total seconds in 24 h:
$$T = 24 \times 3600 = 86400 \text{ s}$$

Step 5: Total memory:
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= 937.5 \times 86400 \\
&= 81{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 81 \text{ MB} \\
&\approx 77.2 \text{ MiB}
\end{aligned}

> **Final Answer:** $\Delta \approx 4.88$ mV; Memory $\approx 81$ MB.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the various types of ambulatory ECG recorders with their clinical applications. Compare continuous Holter, event recorder, and implantable loop recorder.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for QRS detection in Holter analysis. State its key stages and the typical accuracy achieved.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Types of Ambulatory Recorders:**

(Refer to **Section 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix** for detailed comparison.)

1. **Continuous Holter Monitor (24–48 h):**
   - **Principle:** Continuous recording of 2 to 12 leads.
   - **Clinical use:** Detection of frequent arrhythmias, silent ischemia, HRV analysis, QT-interval monitoring.
   - **Limitation:** Short duration; misses events occurring once a week or less.

2. **Event Recorder (Symptom Event Monitor):**
   - **Principle:** Records ECG only when the patient activates it upon feeling symptoms.
   - **Clinical use:** Infrequent palpitations; can be worn for weeks.
   - **Limitation:** Asymptomatic arrhythmias (like silent AF) are missed; depends on patient compliance.

3. **Loop Recorder (External or Implantable):**
   - **Principle:** Maintains a sliding window (e.g., last 5–15 minutes) of continuous ECG in memory; on event (auto-detected or patient-activated), it locks the pre- and post-event data.
   - **Clinical use:** Syncope evaluation, cryptogenic stroke workup, very rare arrhythmias.
   - **Implantable variant (ILR, e.g., Medtronic Reveal LINQ):** Inserted subcutaneously; lasts up to 3 years.

4. **Mobile Cardiac Telemetry (MCT):**
   - **Principle:** Continuous monitoring with real-time transmission to a 24×7 monitoring center.
   - **Clinical use:** High-risk post-MI patients, drug titration studies.

5. **Patch ECG Monitor (e.g., iRhythm Zio):**
   - **Principle:** Single-lead adhesive patch, worn 14 days, no leads or wires.
   - **Clinical use:** Long-term AF detection, replacing the 24-hour Holter in many institutions.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Feature | Holter | Event Recorder | ILR |
|---------|--------|----------------|-----|
| Duration | 24–48 h | Weeks | Up to 3 years |
| Continuous? | Yes | No | Yes (loop) |
| Patient Activated? | Optional | Yes | Both auto + manual |
| Best for | Frequent events | Symptomatic rare events | Very rare / cryptogenic |

---

**Part (b) — Pan-Tompkins Algorithm:**

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm (J. Pan & W. J. Tompkins, 1985) is the **gold standard for real-time QRS detection** in Holter monitors. It is a multi-stage digital signal processing algorithm:

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz):** Isolates the QRS energy from P and T waves, and from baseline wander / EMG noise.

2. **Derivative Operator:** Computes the slope of the ECG to emphasize the steep QRS rising and falling edges.

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{8}(2x[n+1] + x[n] - x[n-1] - 2x[n-2])$$

3. **Squaring Function:** Squares the derivative to make all values positive and to amplify large slopes (QRS) more than small slopes (P, T).

$$y[n] = (x[n])^2$$

4. **Moving Window Integration (MWI):** Smooths the signal over a 150 ms window to find the R-peak envelope:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$

where $N \approx 0.15 \times f_s$.

5. **Adaptive Thresholding:** Two thresholds (one for signal peaks, one for noise) are updated based on the running signal and noise levels.

6. **Search Back & T-wave Discrimination:** If no QRS is detected for > 1.66 s (corresponding to 36 bpm), the algorithm searches back; it also discards peaks that fall within a refractory window of ~200 ms (to ignore T-waves).

> **Reported Performance:** Sensitivity (Se) > 99.5%, Positive Predictivity (+P) > 99.5% on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database (the standard benchmark for ECG algorithms).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** A value of "4.88" without "mV" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing CMRR formula:** Some students write $CMRR = 20 \log(A_d \cdot A_{cm})$ instead of $20 \log(A_d / A_{cm})$. The ratio is **not** the product.
> 3. **Sampling rate confusion:** Students often confuse $f_{max}$ (highest signal frequency) with the sampling rate $f_s$. The answer should always justify $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ with the **Nyquist criterion**.
> 4. **Missing the "clinical justification"** in Holter vs. Event Recorder questions — the examiner expects a comparison based on **event frequency**, not just a list of features.
> 5. **No block diagram in 7-mark design questions:** A question asking for "block diagram" or "functional architecture" without a diagram typically gets only 4–5 out of 7 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the electrode role:** When asked about the signal chain, students often start from the amplifier and forget to mention the **electrode-skin interface impedance** and the **half-cell potential**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **Holter monitor** is a **continuous, ambulatory ECG recorder** used for **24–48 hours**.
- [x] The **inventor was Norman J. Holter (1957)** — a frequently asked KTU objective question.
- [x] Typical ECG amplitude = **0.5–5 mV**; bandwidth = **0.05–150 Hz**; sampling rate = **250–500 Hz** (adult) or **500–1000 Hz** (pediatric).
- [x] **Instrumentation amplifier** is the front-end block: provides high gain, high CMRR (> 80 dB), high input impedance.
- [x] **Bandpass filter 0.05 Hz–150 Hz** is mandatory: 0.05 Hz blocks baseline wander, 150 Hz blocks EMG / aliasing.
- [x] **ADC:** 10–16 bits; quantization step $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^n$.
- [x] **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** is the gold-standard QRS detector.
- [x] **Three major types:** Holter (continuous short-term), Event Recorder (symptom-triggered long-term), ILR (implanted 3-year loop).
- [x] **Patch ECG monitors** (e.g., iRhythm Zio) are replacing 24-hour Holter in many clinical workflows.
- [x] **Statistical basis:** Holter is ~**2880× more sensitive** than a 10-second in-clinic ECG for events occurring once every 8 hours.
- [x] **Memory estimation formula:** $M = n_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot (n/8) \cdot 3600 \cdot T$ bytes (T in hours).
- [x] **Clinical indications:** palpitations, syncope, silent ischemia, post-MI monitoring, AF detection, drug efficacy assessment.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Wearable / patch ECG and MCT systems — expect a question on the difference between traditional Holter and modern patch / telemetry systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sleep Studies: Polysomnography and Sleep Apnea Monitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT504 - ANALYTICAL AND DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sleep Studies: Polysomnography and Sleep Apnea Monitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Polysomnography (PSG) — Formal Academic Definition

**Polysomnography (PSG)** is a comprehensive, multi-parametric, continuous recording of the biophysiological changes that occur during sleep. As defined by the American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) — the global standard adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus — a full clinical polysomnogram simultaneously acquires **electroencephalogram (EEG)**, **electro-oculogram (EOG)**, **submental electromyogram (EMG)**, **electrocardiogram (ECG)**, **respiratory airflow**, **respiratory effort**, **pulse oximetry (SpO₂)**, and **body position** signals throughout an entire night of sleep (typically 6–8 hours).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PSG is the *gold-standard* diagnostic procedure for sleep-disordered breathing (SDB), narcolepsy, parasomnias, and periodic limb movement disorder. The classical study is called a **Type I attended, in-laboratory polysomnogram**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Night-Watch Conductor"

Imagine an orchestra playing a symphony in pitch darkness. As the listener (the clinician), you cannot see the musicians, but you want to know exactly which instrument is sounding, how loud, and in what rhythm throughout the entire performance.

Polysomnography is like placing a **high-fidelity microphone on every single musician** (brain waves, eye movements, muscle tone, breathing, heart, oxygen) and recording the entire night-long concert onto a multi-track tape. The next morning, the sleep physician "re-plays" the night and reads the score — measuring sleep stages, apneas, arousals, and desaturation events with millisecond precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> - **PSG Channels (Standard AASM):** EEG (≥ 3 derivations), EOG (2 channels), Chin EMG, Leg EMG, ECG, Snore microphone, Nasal pressure transducer, Thoracic & abdominal effort belts, Pulse oximeter, Body position sensor.
> - **Sampling Rate:** EEG/EOG/EMG = **200–500 Hz**; Respiratory & SpO₂ = **10–25 Hz**.
> - **Recording Duration:** Minimum **6 hours** of valid sleep time for a diagnostic study.

### 1.3 Sleep Apnea Monitors — Formal Definition

A **Sleep Apnea Monitor** is a dedicated biomedical instrument designed to detect, quantify, and record episodes of **cessation (apnea)** or **shallow reduction (hypopnea)** of breathing during sleep. These range from full laboratory PSG systems to **Home Sleep Test (HST)** devices (Type II, III, IV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Definition (AASM 2017 Scoring Manual):**
> - **Apnea:** ≥ 90% drop in peak airflow signal lasting ≥ 10 seconds.
> - **Hypopnea (Recommended rule):** ≥ 30% drop in airflow with ≥ 3% oxygen desaturation **OR** an EEG arousal, lasting ≥ 10 seconds.
> - **Obstructive Apnea:** Apnea with continued respiratory effort (the airway is blocked, but the brain still commands breathing).
> - **Central Apnea:** Apnea with **absent** respiratory effort (the brain temporarily fails to send the command).
> - **Mixed Apnea:** Begins as central, ends as obstructive.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy for Sleep Apnea

Think of a **vacuum cleaner hose** with a kink in it. A **central apnea** is like the *electricity going off* — the motor stops because the wall switch (brain) opened; no effort is made. An **obstructive apnea** is like the hose being *pinched by a heavy book* — the motor is still running hard (diaphragm is straining), but no air gets through because the path is physically blocked. A **mixed apnea** is the electricity briefly failing, then returning, but the hose is still pinched.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Polysomnography System

A clinical PSG system is organized into **four functional tiers**:

1. **Tier 1 — Sensors / Transducers Layer**
   - Electrode-skin interface (Ag/AgCl) → EEG, EOG, EMG, ECG
   - Pressure transducer (nasal cannula) → airflow
   - Piezoelectric / inductive belts → thoracic & abdominal effort
   - Photoplethysmography (PPG) probe → SpO₂
   - Microphone / vibration sensor → snoring

2. **Tier 2 — Analog Signal Conditioning Layer**
   - Differential instrumentation amplifiers (CMRR ≥ 100 dB)
   - Bandpass filters (EEG: 0.5–35 Hz; EOG: 0.5–30 Hz; EMG: 10–100 Hz)
   - 50/60 Hz notch filter for power-line rejection
   - Isolation amplifiers for patient safety (IEC 60601-1)

3. **Tier 3 — Digital Conversion & Processing**
   - 16–24 bit ADC at sampling rates above the Nyquist limit
   - Real-time digital filtering
   - Epoch segmentation (the record is divided into **30-second windows** called *epochs*)

4. **Tier 4 — Display, Storage & Scoring**
   - Hypnogram generation (a graph of sleep stages vs. time)
   - Automated event detection (apneas, hypopneas, desaturations, arousals)
   - PDF report generation

### 2.2 Sleep Staging Logic (Rechtschaffen & Kales → AASM)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Sleep is scored in **30-second epochs** and classified into stages:
> - **W** (Wake)
> - **N1** — Non-REM Stage 1 (sleep onset, 5–10% of TST)
> - **N2** — Non-REM Stage 2 (light sleep, ~45% of TST)
> - **N3** — Non-REM Stage 3 (Slow-Wave Sleep, deep sleep, ~20% of TST)
> - **R** — REM (Rapid Eye Movement, ~25% of TST)

The scoring is based on three simultaneous inputs:
- **EEG** (brain waves)
- **EOG** (eye movements)
- **Chin EMG** (muscle tone)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula or Criteria | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) | AHI = (N_apnea + N_hypopnea) / T_sleep | events/hour | **Severity of sleep apnea** |
| Respiratory Disturbance Index (RDI) | RDI = (Apnea + Hypopnea + RERA) / T_sleep | events/hour | Includes Respiratory Effort-Related Arousals |
| Oxygen Desaturation Index (ODI) | ODI = N_desat_events / T_sleep | events/hour | Frequency of ≥ 3–4% SpO₂ drops |
| Total Sleep Time (TST) | TST = ∑(Epochs_N1 + N2 + N3 + R) × 30 s | hours | Denominator for all indices |
| Sleep Efficiency (SE) | SE = (TST / T_in_bed) × 100 | % | Quality of sleep |
| Arousal Index (AI) | AI = N_arousals / T_sleep | events/hour | Sleep fragmentation |
| Apnea duration criterion | t_apnea ≥ 10 s | seconds | Minimum to score apnea |
| Hypopnea duration criterion | t_hypopnea ≥ 10 s | seconds | Minimum to score hypopnea |
| Desaturation criterion | ΔSpO₂ ≥ 3% (AASM 2017) | % | Required for hypopnea scoring |
| SaO₂ nadir (lowest) | min[SpO₂(t)] over event | % | Severity marker |
| AHI Severity (AASM) | Normal: AHI \vert 5; Mild: 5–14.9; Moderate: 15–29.9; Severe: ≥ 30 | events/hour | Diagnosis of OSA |
| Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | f_s ≥ 2 × f_signal_max | Hz | Prevents aliasing |
| Pressure transducer sensitivity | S = ΔV / ΔP | V/Pa or V/cmH₂O | Airflow measurement |
| Pulse oximeter (Beer-Lambert) | I = I₀ · e^(–ε·c·d) | a.u. | Beer–Lambert absorption law |
| Polysomnograph CMRR | CMRR = 20·log₁₀(A_d / A_cm) | dB | ≥ 100 dB typical |
| Epoch duration (scoring) | t_epoch = 30 s | seconds | Standard AASM window |

### 2.4 Clinical Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **Diagnosis of Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)**: The AHI calculated from PSG is the WHO-recognized diagnostic standard. Engineers design auto-titrating **CPAP machines** that use PSG-derived pressure data.
- **Titration Studies (CPAP/BiPAP)**: PSG is used to determine the optimal positive airway pressure.
- **Pre-surgical evaluation** for uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) or maxillomandibular advancement.
- **Neurological diagnosis** of narcolepsy (Multiple Sleep Latency Test, MSLT).
- **Portable/wearable sleep trackers** (consumer-grade) are validated against PSG as the gold reference.

### 2.5 Sleep Apnea Monitor Subsystems

A modern **portable Type III Home Sleep Test (HST)** device typically records:
1. **Nasal airflow** (thermistor or pressure cannula)
2. **Thoracic effort** (inductive plethysmography belt)
3. **Abdominal effort** (inductive plethysmography belt)
4. **SpO₂** (fingertip pulse oximeter)
5. **Heart rate / pulse**
6. **Body position** (3-axis accelerometer)
7. **Snore** (acoustic or vibration sensor)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Understand the *difference between thermistor and nasal pressure cannula*. The thermistor (or thermocouple) senses **temperature change** of inspired (ambient) vs. expired (warm) air — a qualitative binary signal. The nasal pressure cannula uses a **differential pressure transducer** to quantify airflow *quantitatively*, making it the AASM-recommended sensor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Apnea–Hypopnea Index (AHI)

The AHI is the central metric in sleep medicine. Its derivation from raw signal data is as follows:

**Step 1 — Acquire respiratory signal.**
Let $a(t)$ denote the peak nasal pressure signal amplitude (in cmH₂O or arbitrary units) sampled at $f_s = 25$ Hz over a full night recording of total duration $T_{\text{rec}}$ (in hours).

**Step 2 — Baseline normal breathing amplitude.**
For each moving 120-second window centered at time $t$, compute the baseline amplitude:

$$A_{\text{base}}(t) = \text{median}\bigl\{a(\tau)\;:\;\vert t - \tau \vert \le 60\,\text{s}\bigr\}$$

This robust estimator ignores short apneas during its own calculation.

**Step 3 — Detect candidate apnea events.**
An apnea is flagged when the signal drops to less than **10%** of baseline:

$$\frac{a(t)}{A_{\text{base}}(t)} < 0.10 \quad \text{for a duration} \quad \Delta t \ge 10\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Detect hypopnea events.**
A hypopnea is flagged when:

$$0.10 \le \frac{a(t)}{A_{\text{base}}(t)} \le 0.70 \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta t \ge 10\,\text{s} \quad \text{AND} \quad \Delta\text{SpO}_2 \ge 3\%$$

**Step 5 — Classify apnea subtype (Obstructive vs. Central).**
Let $e_{\text{th}}(t)$ and $e_{\text{ab}}(t)$ be the thoracic and abdominal effort belt signals. The sum (paradoxical index) is:

$$P(t) = e_{\text{th}}(t) + e_{\text{ab}}(t)$$

Then:
- **Obstructive** apnea → $P(t)$ is **not flat**; effort continues.
- **Central** apnea → $P(t)$ is **flat** (no effort).
- **Mixed** apnea → $P(t)$ flat for first part, resumes for the rest.

**Step 6 — Compute AHI.**
Let $N_{\text{apnea}}$ and $N_{\text{hypopnea}}$ be the total counts of all apnea and hypopnea events during the recording, and $T_{\text{sleep}}$ the total sleep time in hours. Then:

$$\boxed{\text{AHI} = \frac{N_{\text{apnea}} + N_{\text{hypopnea}}}{T_{\text{sleep}}}}$$

**Step 7 — Worked numerical example.**
A patient slept for $T_{\text{sleep}} = 7.0$ h. The night log shows:
- Obstructive apneas: 35
- Central apneas: 5
- Mixed apneas: 2
- Hypopneas: 58

$$N_{\text{apnea}} = 35 + 5 + 2 = 42$$
$$N_{\text{hypopnea}} = 58$$
$$\text{AHI} = \frac{42 + 58}{7.0} = \frac{100}{7.0} \approx 14.3 \;\text{events/hour}$$

> **Result:** $\text{AHI} \approx 14.3$ → classified as **Mild-to-Moderate** Obstructive Sleep Apnea by AASM criteria.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Pulse-Oximetry Oxygen Saturation Equation

SpO₂ sensors use the **Beer–Lambert Law** of optical absorption. Two wavelengths are used: **Red (≈ 660 nm)** and **Infrared (≈ 940 nm)**.

The transmitted intensities through the finger are:

$$I_R = I_{0R} \cdot e^{-\varepsilon_R \cdot c \cdot d}$$
$$I_{IR} = I_{0IR} \cdot e^{-\varepsilon_{IR} \cdot c \cdot d}$$

The **ratio of ratios (R)** is empirically formed as:

$$R = \frac{\log_{10}(I_{0R}/I_R)}{\log_{10}(I_{0IR}/I_{IR})} = \frac{\varepsilon_R \cdot c \cdot d}{\varepsilon_{IR} \cdot c \cdot d} = \frac{\varepsilon_R}{\varepsilon_{IR}}$$

A **calibration curve** then maps $R$ to SpO₂:

$$\text{SpO}_2 \;[\%] = f(R) \quad \text{(empirically tabulated in firmware)}$$

A typical linearized relationship in commercial devices is:

$$\text{SpO}_2 \approx 110 - 25 \cdot R \quad \text{for } R \in [0.4,\, 1.0]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** This is an empirical, *not* universal relationship. The exact coefficients vary by manufacturer and are patented.

### 3.3 Derivation of Sleep Efficiency

Let $T_{\text{TRT}}$ = Total Recording Time, $T_{\text{sleep}}$ = Total Sleep Time, $T_{\text{awake}}$ = Total Awake Time after sleep onset.

Then:

$$T_{\text{sleep}} = T_{\text{TRT}} - T_{\text{awake}}$$
$$\text{SE} = \frac{T_{\text{sleep}}}{T_{\text{TRT}}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Example:** A patient was in bed for 8 h, but took 25 min to fall asleep and was awake for another 35 min during the night.

$$T_{\text{sleep}} = 8.0 \text{ h} - 0.417 \text{ h} - 0.583 \text{ h} = 7.0 \text{ h}$$
$$\text{SE} = \frac{7.0}{8.0} \times 100 = 87.5\,\%$$

This is considered **normal** (SE > 85%).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sleep Stage Identification Rules

| EEG Feature | Frequency / Pattern | Stage | Physiological Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| Alpha rhythm (8–13 Hz) | Eyes closed, relaxed wake | W | Posterior dominant rhythm |
| Theta waves (4–7 Hz) | Slow rolling eye movements, low EMG | N1 | Sleep onset |
| Sleep spindles (12–14 Hz, ≥ 0.5 s) + K-complexes | Sharp biphasic waves | N2 | Thalamocortical oscillations |
| Delta waves (0.5–4 Hz, ≥ 75 µV) | ≥ 20% of epoch | N3 | Slow-wave (deep) sleep |
| Sawtooth theta + low chin EMG + REMs | Rapid eye movements | R | Dreaming, muscle atonia |
| Low-voltage mixed frequency (LVMF) | No spindles, no delta | N1/N2 boundary | Transitional |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Full Polysomnography System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PATIENT ON BED] --> B1[EEG Electrodes Fpz Cz Oz]
    A --> B2[EOG Left and Right]
    A --> B3[Chin EMG]
    A --> B4[Leg EMG Tibialis]
    A --> B5[Nasal Pressure Cannula]
    A --> B6[Thoracic Effort Belt]
    A --> B7[Abdominal Effort Belt]
    A --> B8[SpO2 Finger Probe]
    A --> B9[Snore Microphone]
    A --> B10[ECG Leads]
    A --> B11[Body Position Sensor]

    B1 --> C1[Instrumentation Amplifier 0.5 to 35 Hz]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2[EMG Amp 10 to 100 Hz]
    B4 --> C2
    B5 --> C3[Pressure Transducer]
    B6 --> C4[Piezoelectric Amplifier]
    B7 --> C4
    B8 --> C5[PPG Analog Front End]
    B9 --> C6[Microphone Preamp]
    B10 --> C7[ECG Amp 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B11 --> C8[3 Axis Accelerometer]

    C1 --> D1[16 to 24 bit ADC]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1
    C7 --> D1
    C8 --> D1

    D1 --> E[Digital Signal Processor]
    E --> F1[Real Time Display]
    E --> F2[Automated Event Detection]
    E --> F3[Epoch Staging 30 s]
    E --> F4[Hypnogram Generator]
    F3 --> G[Sleep Physician Scoring]
    F2 --> G
    F4 --> H[Final PDF Report AHI RDI ODI]
```

### 4.2 Sensor Placement on the Patient (10–20 EEG System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HEAD[SCALP 10 to 20 ELECTRODE MONTAGE]
        H1[FPz Forehead]
        H2[Cz Central Vertex]
        H3[Oz Occipital]
        H4[F3 Left Frontal]
        H5[F4 Right Frontal]
        H6[C3 Left Central]
        H7[C4 Right Central]
        H8[O1 Left Occipital]
        H9[O2 Right Occipital]
        H10[A1 Left Ear Reference]
        H11[A2 Right Ear Reference]
    end

    subgraph EYES[EOG PLACEMENT]
        E1[E1 One cm above right outer canthus]
        E2[E2 One cm below left outer canthus]
    end

    subgraph CHIN[CHIN EMG TRIPLODE]
        M1[Chin1 Mentalis]
        M2[Chin2 Submentalis]
        M3[Chin3 Submentalis]
    end

    subgraph BODY[BODY SENSORS]
        B1[Nose Cannula for Airflow]
        B2[Chest Belt Thoracic Effort]
        B3[Abdomen Belt Abdominal Effort]
        B4[Finger Probe for SpO2]
        B5[ECG Lead II]
        B6[Leg EMG on Anterior Tibialis]
    end

    H1 --- H2
    H2 --- H3
```

### 4.3 Sleep Apnea Event Detection Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Recording Loop] --> B[Acquire 30 s Epoch]
    B --> C{Is Nasal Pressure Signal}
    C -->|Drop ≥ 90 percent| D[Apnea Candidate]
    C -->|Drop 30 to 70 percent| E[Hypopnea Candidate]
    C -->|Drop under 10 percent| F[No Event]

    D --> G{Does Effort Belt Continue}
    G -->|Yes| H[Obstructive Apnea Counter +1]
    G -->|No| I{Is Effort Absent at Start}
    I -->|Yes then Resumes| J[Mixed Apnea Counter +1]
    I -->|Absent Whole Time| K[Central Apnea Counter +1]

    E --> L{SpO2 Drop ≥ 3 percent}
    L -->|Yes| M[Hypopnea Counter +1]
    L -->|No| N{Arousal in EEG}
    N -->|Yes| M
    N -->|No| F

    H --> O[Compute AHI at End of Night]
    J --> O
    K --> O
    M --> O
    F --> O
    O --> A
```

### 4.4 CPAP Therapy Signal Flow (used in Titration PSG)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Upper Airway] --> B[Collapsible Pharyngeal Segment]
    B --> C{Nasal Mask Interface}
    C --> D[Blower Motor with Variable Speed]
    D --> E[Pressure Transducer Feedback]
    E --> F[Microcontroller PID Loop]
    F -->|RPM command| D
    F --> G[Display Set Pressure cmH2O]
    F --> H[Humidifier Chamber Heated]
    H --> A
    I[Auto CPAP Algorithm Reads Snore and Flow Limitation] --> F
    J[PSG Lab Tech Monitors on Laptop] --> K[Adjusts Pressure Remotely]
```
