| Budget Compliance (C4) | 0.20 | Total cost ≤ ₹ 10,000 |
| Regulatory Awareness (C5) | 0.10 | Must cite at least one IEC/ISO standard |

**Step 2 — Score each topic on a 1–10 scale (subjective, but justified):**

| Topic | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1: Wearable ECG with Bluetooth streaming | 9 | 8 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| T2: Smartphone-based photoplethysmography (PPG) heart-rate monitor | 8 | 9 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| T3: Non-invasive blood glucose monitor using NIR | 9 | 4 | 10 | 3 | 6 |

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted score for each topic:**

The weighted score is:

$$
S_T = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \times s_i
$$

**For Topic T1 (Wearable ECG):**

$$
S_{T1} = (0.25 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9)
$$

$$
= 2.25 + 2.40 + 1.05 + 1.60 + 0.90
$$

$$
S_{T1} = 8.20
$$

**For Topic T2 (Smartphone PPG):**

$$
S_{T2} = (0.25 \times 8) + (0.30 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7)
$$

$$
= 2.00 + 2.70 + 0.90 + 1.80 + 0.70
$$

$$
S_{T2} = 8.10
$$

**For Topic T3 (NIR Glucometer):**

$$
S_{T3} = (0.25 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 10) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 6)
$$

$$
= 2.25 + 1.20 + 1.50 + 0.60 + 0.60
$$

$$
S_{T3} = 6.15
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

| Rank | Topic | Score | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T1 — Wearable ECG | 8.20 | ✅ SELECTED |
| 2 | T2 — Smartphone PPG | 8.10 | ❌ Reserve (alternate) |
| 3 | T3 — NIR Glucometer | 6.15 | ❌ Reject (low technical feasibility) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** T3 had the highest novelty (10/10) but failed on technical feasibility (4/10) and budget (3/10). The weighted score correctly penalised it. This is exactly the kind of *defensible, quantitative decision* the examiner expects in a project proposal viva.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Bill of Materials (BOM) & Budget Compliance

The team chooses **T1: Wearable ECG**. They must now produce a BOM costing ≤ ₹ 10,000.

| # | Component | Specification | Qty | Unit Price (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AD8232 ECG front-end IC | 2-electrode, integrated | 1 | 850 | 850 |
| 2 | ESP32-WROOM-32 | Wi-Fi + BLE module | 1 | 450 | 450 |
| 3 | ECG electrodes (Ag/AgCl) | 3-lead, disposable pack of 50 | 1 | 600 | 600 |
| 4 | 16-bit ADC — ADS1115 | I²C, 860 SPS | 1 | 350 | 350 |
| 5 | 3.7 V Li-Po battery, 1000 mAh | With TP4056 charger | 1 | 250 | 250 |
| 6 | 0.96" OLED display (I²C) | 128 × 64 | 1 | 200 | 200 |
| 7 | Custom PCB (JLCPCB, 5 pcs) | 2-layer, 50 × 50 mm | 1 lot | 1,200 | 1,200 |
| 8 | 3D-printed enclosure (PLA) | Ergonomic, 50 g | 1 | 800 | 800 |
| 9 | Misc. (resistors, caps, connectors) | Passives | 1 lot | 500 | 500 |
| 10 | Wrist strap + buckles | Standard | 1 | 300 | 300 |

**Total BOM cost:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{total}} = 850 + 450 + 600 + 350 + 250 + 200 + 1{,}200 + 800 + 500 + 300
$$

$$
= 5{,}500 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Budget headroom:**

$$
\text{Headroom} = \text{Budget} - \text{Cost}_{\text{total}} = 10{,}000 - 5{,}500 = 4{,}500 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Utilization:**

$$
\eta_{\text{budget}} = \frac{5{,}500}{10{,}000} \times 100 \% = 55 \%
$$

This 45 % headroom is healthy — it allows for component price fluctuations, replacement parts, and shipping costs.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** A BOM that uses **> 95 % of the budget** signals poor planning. A BOM at **50–70 % utilization** with justified headroom signals maturity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Gantt Chart for 4-Month Timeline

The project duration is **16 weeks** (≈ 4 months). Tasks are mapped to phases with dependencies (Finish-to-Start, FS).

| Task ID | Task | Phase | Duration (weeks) | Predecessor | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Topic finalization & proposal | 1 | 1 | — | Team Lead |
| T2 | Literature review (≥ 15 papers) | 2 | 3 | T1 | Member A |
| T3 | Specification table | 3 | 1 | T2 | Member B |
| T4 | Component procurement | 4 | 2 | T3 | Member C |
| T5 | Schematic & PCB design | 4 | 2 | T3 | Member B |
| T6 | Firmware development (ESP32) | 4 | 3 | T5 | Member D |
| T7 | 3D-printed enclosure | 4 | 2 | T3 | Member C |
| T8 | Subsystem unit testing | 5 | 1 | T4, T5, T6 | Member A |
| T9 | Integration & bring-up | 5 | 1 | T8 | Member B |
| T10 | Validation on volunteers (n = 5) | 5 | 2 | T9 | Member C |
| T11 | Final report writing | 5 | 2 | T10 | Team Lead |
| T12 | Presentation prep & demo | 5 | 1 | T11 | All |

**Critical path (longest sequence):** T1 → T2 → T3 → T5 → T6 → T8 → T9 → T10 → T11 → T12

**Total critical-path duration:**

$$
T_{\text{critical}} = 1 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 17 \text{ weeks}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The critical path is **17 weeks**, which **exceeds the 16-week semester** by 1 week. The team must either (a) parallelise T2 and T3 (do them simultaneously) to recover 1 week, or (b) request a 1-week extension. **KTU's project coordinator will not accept a missed deadline without evidence of a documented mitigation plan.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: FMEA on the Wearable ECG

The team identifies **5 potential failure modes** in the wearable ECG.

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrode detachment mid-recording | Data loss | Sweat, motion | 7 | 5 | 3 | 105 |
| 2 | ADC saturation on motion artifact | Clipped QRS | High-amplitude EMG | 6 | 6 | 4 | 144 |
| 3 | Battery over-discharge | Device shutdown | No low-battery cutoff | 8 | 3 | 2 | 48 |
| 4 | BLE disconnection | Loss of streaming | Range > 10 m | 5 | 7 | 3 | 105 |
| 5 | Software infinite loop | Device hang | No watchdog timer | 9 | 4 | 2 | 72 |

**RPN calculation (worked for row 2):**

$$
\text{RPN}_2 = S_2 \times O_2 \times D_2 = 6 \times 6 \times 4 = 144
$$

**Highest-risk failure:** Row 2 (RPN = 144 > threshold of 100). The team must add a **band-pass filter (0.5–40 Hz)** and a **software saturation detector** that pauses logging and alerts the user.

**Re-scored RPN after mitigation:**

| # | S' | O' | D' | RPN' |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 | 3 | 2 | 42 |
| 2 | 6 | 3 | 2 | 36 |
| 3 | 8 | 1 | 1 | 8 |
| 4 | 5 | 4 | 2 | 40 |
| 5 | 9 | 1 | 1 | 9 |

All RPN' ≤ 50 → **Risk is acceptable** per ISO 14971.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Bland-Altman Validation of Wearable ECG vs. Reference Monitor

**Scenario:** The team tests their wearable ECG on **N = 10** healthy volunteers, simultaneously with a **Philips Intellivue MX450** reference monitor. They compare the **R-R interval** (a measure of heart-rate variability, in milliseconds).

| Volunteer $i$ | Wearable $x_i$ (ms) | Reference $y_i$ (ms) | Difference $d_i = x_i - y_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 820 | 815 | +5 |
| 2 | 760 | 770 | −10 |
| 3 | 900 | 905 | −5 |
| 4 | 680 | 690 | −10 |
| 5 | 850 | 845 | +5 |
| 6 | 720 | 715 | +5 |
| 7 | 810 | 800 | +10 |
| 8 | 880 | 885 | −5 |
| 9 | 740 | 745 | −5 |
| 10 | 800 | 805 | −5 |

**Step 1 — Compute the mean difference (bias):**

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i
$$

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{(+5) + (-10) + (-5) + (-10) + (+5) + (+5) + (+10) + (-5) + (-5) + (-5)}{10}
$$

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{-15}{10} = -1.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the standard deviation of the differences:**

$$
s_d = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - \bar{d})^2}
$$

Compute each squared deviation $(d_i - \bar{d})^2$:

| $i$ | $d_i$ | $d_i - \bar{d} = d_i + 1.5$ | $(d_i - \bar{d})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +5 | 6.5 | 42.25 |
| 2 | −10 | −8.5 | 72.25 |
| 3 | −5 | −3.5 | 12.25 |
| 4 | −10 | −8.5 | 72.25 |
| 5 | +5 | 6.5 | 42.25 |
| 6 | +5 | 6.5 | 42.25 |
| 7 | +10 | 11.5 | 132.25 |
| 8 | −5 | −3.5 | 12.25 |
| 9 | −5 | −3.5 | 12.25 |
| 10 | −5 | −3.5 | 12.25 |

**Sum of squared deviations:**

$$
\sum (d_i - \bar{d})^2 = 42.25 + 72.25 + 12.25 + 72.25 + 42.25 + 42.25 + 132.25 + 12.25 + 12.25 + 12.25
$$

$$
= 452.50
$$

**Standard deviation:**

$$
s_d = \sqrt{\frac{452.50}{10 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{452.50}{9}} = \sqrt{50.278} \approx 7.09 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the 95 % limits of agreement:**

$$
\text{LLA} = \bar{d} - 1.96 \, s_d = -1.5 - 1.96 \times 7.09 = -1.5 - 13.90 = -15.40 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\text{ULA} = \bar{d} + 1.96 \, s_d = -1.5 + 1.96 \times 7.09 = -1.5 + 13.90 = +12.40 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance check:**

The clinically acceptable error band for R-R interval is typically **± 20 ms**. Since LLA = −15.4 ms and ULA = +12.4 ms are both **within ± 20 ms**, the wearable ECG is **clinically interchangeable** with the reference monitor for R-R interval measurement.

**Interpretation for the report:**

> *"The wearable ECG demonstrated a mean bias of −1.5 ms (95 % CI of agreement: −15.4 to +12.4 ms) against the Philips Intellivue MX450. Since the limits of agreement are within the clinically acceptable ± 20 ms band for R-R interval, the device may be considered equivalent to the reference for HRV monitoring applications."*

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Literature Review (Worked Example)

The team's literature review identifies **5 prior works** on wearable ECG. They summarize them in a comparative table:

| # | Reference (IEEE format) | Sensor / Front-end | Wireless | Algorithm | Sample Size | Limitation Identified |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | R. Kumar et al., *"Low-cost wearable ECG..."*, IEEE EMBC 2022 | AD8232 | BLE 4.2 | Pan-Tompkins | n = 20, healthy | No motion artifact compensation |
| [2] | S. Liu et al., *"Smart-textile ECG shirt"*, IEEE TBME 2021 | Textile electrodes | Wi-Fi | Wavelet denoise | n = 15 | Skin-electrode impedance unstable |
| [3] | M. Patel et al., *"AI-enabled ECG patch"*, Sensors 2023 | Dry electrodes | LoRa | CNN classifier | n = 50, arrhythmia | High power consumption |
| [4] | A. George et al., *"ECG on ESP32"*, ICCCNT 2022 | Disposable Ag/AgCl | Wi-Fi | Moving average | n = 10 | No QRS detection algorithm |
| [5] | WHO Technical Report 2020, *"mHealth for CVD"*, WHO Press | — | — | — | — | No specific design |

**Research gap statement (synthesized by the team):**

> *"None of the surveyed works combine a low-cost (< ₹ 6,000) AD8232 + ESP32 architecture with both a Pan-Tompkins QRS detector AND a software-based motion-artifact compensation algorithm, validated against a clinical-grade reference on Indian subjects. Our project fills this gap."*

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Define "Topic Selection" in the context of a biomedical instrumentation project. List any three criteria used in the feasibility matrix.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Topic Selection is the **first phase** of the biomedical instrumentation project lifecycle in which the student team identifies, evaluates, and finalizes a clinically relevant engineering problem to be solved within the constraints of the semester, the team size, and the available budget. The output is a **1-page project charter** containing the problem statement, PICO formulation, and a weighted feasibility score.

**Three criteria of the feasibility matrix (1.5 marks):**

1. **Clinical Relevance (weight ≈ 0.25)** — Does the topic address a real unmet clinical need?
2. **Technical Feasibility (weight ≈ 0.30)** — Can the design be completed within 4 months by a B.Tech team?
3. **Budget Compliance (weight ≈ 0.20)** — Is the total BOM ≤ ₹ 10,000–15,000?

*(Other acceptable criteria: Innovation/Novelty, Regulatory Awareness.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**Q: What is a Bland-Altman plot? Why is it preferred over simple correlation analysis for validating a new biomedical device?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Bland-Altman plot** is a scatter plot of the **difference** between two paired measurements (new device − reference) on the y-axis versus the **mean** of the two measurements on the x-axis. It is supplemented by three horizontal lines representing the **mean bias $\bar{d}$**, the **lower 95 % limit of agreement (LLA = $\bar{d} - 1.96 s_d$)**, and the **upper 95 % limit of agreement (ULA = $\bar{d} + 1.96 s_d$)**.

**Why preferred over correlation (1.5 marks):**

1. Correlation only measures **linear association**, not **agreement** — two devices can be perfectly correlated yet have a constant bias.
2. Bland-Altman directly quantifies the **systematic bias** and the **random error spread**, which is what clinicians care about.
3. It allows a direct **visual check** of whether the limits of agreement lie within a **pre-specified clinical acceptance band** (e.g., ± 2 % for SpO₂, ± 3 mmHg for NIBP).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B questions for this module typically have **internal choice**, meaning the student answers **EITHER Question A OR Question B**. Each question is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The model answers below are exhaustive step-by-step solutions modeled on past KTU board examiner key patterns.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **five phases of a team-based biomedical instrumentation project** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. For each phase, list one **deliverable** and one **gate-review criterion**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A team of four B.Tech students wants to design a **portable pulse oximeter** for rural primary health centres. Apply the **PICO framework** to scope the problem and the **Feasibility Matrix** to evaluate the topic. Justify the chosen weights. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) The Five Phases (7 marks — 1.4 marks per phase)**

| Phase | Deliverable | Gate-Review Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Topic Selection** | 1-page project charter with PICO statement | Guide approves the topic and signed charter is filed |
| **2. Literature Review** | 8–15 page IEEE-style report with ≥ 15 references and a comparative table | Comparative table shows ≥ 5 prior works; gap statement is approved |
| **3. Analysis of Working Principle, Characteristics & Application Considerations** | Design specification table with numerical targets (range, accuracy, bandwidth, CMRR) and the applicable IEC/ISO standard | Specification is signed off by guide and frozen for the design phase |
| **4. Design, Development & Prototyping** | Working prototype (breadboard / PCB) with firmware and 3D-printed enclosure | Mid-semester demo shows the prototype acquiring live physiological data |
| **5. Testing, Validation & Documentation** | Test report with unit-test results, Bland-Altman plot, FMEA table, and bound final report | External examiner conducts viva-voce and evaluates the prototype live |

**Key concepts to mention in the answer:**

- The phases follow a **waterfall-V model** mapped to **ISO 13485 design control clause 7.3**.
- Each gate review is a **documented checkpoint** — failure to pass a gate blocks progression to the next phase.
- The phases are **iterative in practice**: findings in Phase 5 (e.g., accuracy not met) feed back to Phase 3 (tighten specification) and Phase 4 (re-design).

**(b) PICO + Feasibility Matrix (7 marks)**

**PICO Statement (2 marks):**

- **P (Population):** Adults aged 30–70 attending rural primary health centres (PHCs) in Kerala for routine check-ups, suspected of hypoxemia.
- **I (Intervention):** A portable, battery-operated, finger-tip pulse oximeter (SpO₂ + heart-rate) costing < ₹ 5,000, with Bluetooth streaming to a smartphone.
- **C (Comparator):** Standard tabletop pulse oximeter (e.g., Contec CMS50DL) used at PHCs, accuracy ± 2 %.
- **O (Outcome):** Achieve SpO₂ accuracy of ± 2 % over 70–100 % range, ± 2 bpm heart-rate accuracy, ≥ 8 hours battery life, weight < 100 g.

**Feasibility Matrix (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Justification | Score (1–10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clinical Relevance (C1) | 0.25 | Hypoxemia detection is critical in pneumonia, COVID-19, and post-op care | 9 |
| Technical Feasibility (C2) | 0.30 | MAX30102 + ESP32 is well-documented in hobbyist literature; buildable in 4 months | 9 |
| Innovation / Novelty (C3) | 0.15 | Adding Bluetooth streaming to a smartphone app differentiates from off-the-shelf units | 7 |
| Budget Compliance (C4) | 0.20 | MAX30102 (₹ 400) + ESP32 (₹ 450) + OLED + 3D print ≈ ₹ 4,000 total | 9 |
| Regulatory Awareness (C5) | 0.10 | Must cite ISO 80601-2-61 (particular standard for pulse oximeters) | 8 |

**Weighted score:**

$$
S_T = (0.25 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_T = 2.25 + 2.70 + 1.05 + 1.80 + 0.80 = 8.60
$$

**Justification of weights (1 mark):** Technical Feasibility gets the highest weight (0.30) because project failure is most often due to under-scoped technical ambition. Clinical Relevance and Budget Compliance are tied at 0.25 and 0.20 because both are essential but not the leading cause of failure. Innovation and Regulatory Awareness receive lower weights because the project is academic, not commercial.

**Conclusion:** $S_T = 8.60 \geq 7.0$ → **Topic selected and approved.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define the **FMEA** methodology. A team has identified that a **battery over-discharge** in their wearable ECG prototype has Severity = 8, Occurrence = 3, Detectability = 2. Compute the **RPN**. If the threshold for mitigation is RPN ≥ 100, propose **two design controls** to reduce it below 50. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During validation, a team recorded R-R intervals from their wearable ECG and a Philips Intellivue MX450 on 8 volunteers. The differences $d_i$ (in ms) are: {+4, −6, −2, +8, −3, +5, −7, +1}. Compute the **mean bias $\bar{d}$**, the **standard deviation $s_d$**, and the **95 % limits of agreement**. State whether the device is clinically interchangeable with the reference (acceptance band: ± 15 ms). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) FMEA + RPN (7 marks)**

**Definition of FMEA (2 marks):** **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive methodology (originating in the US military MIL-STD-1629 and now standardized in **IEC 60812** and **ISO 14971**) used to identify potential failure modes of a product/process, assess their **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detectability (D)**, compute a **Risk Priority Number (RPN = S × O × D)**, and prioritize mitigation actions for high-RPN items.

**Given:** $S = 8$, $O = 3$, $D = 2$.

**RPN computation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 3 \times 2 = 48
$$

**Threshold check (1 mark):** Since RPN = 48 < 100, the failure mode is **below the mandatory mitigation threshold**. However, the team may still choose to mitigate it as **best practice**, especially because **Severity = 8 is high** (battery over-discharge could cause device shutdown during patient monitoring).

**Two design controls to reduce RPN to < 50 (3 marks):**

1. **Hardware mitigation — Battery Management IC (e.g., DW01A + FS8205):** Add a dedicated lithium-battery protection IC that cuts off discharge at 2.5 V cell voltage. This reduces **Occurrence** from $O = 3$ to $O = 1$ (very rare event).
2. **Firmware mitigation — Low-battery alarm and graceful shutdown:** The ESP32 firmware reads the battery voltage via ADC every 10 seconds. If $V_{\text{bat}} < 3.3$ V, the device beeps and flashes a low-battery icon, and if $V_{\text{bat}} < 3.0$ V, the device saves logs and shuts down cleanly. This reduces **Detectability** from $D = 2$ to $D = 1$ (failure is now detected well before patient harm).

**Re-scored RPN:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 8 \times 1 \times 1 = 8
$$

Both controls bring the RPN from 48 to **8**, well below the 50 target.

**(b) Bland-Altman Analysis (7 marks)**

**Given data:**

| $i$ | $d_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|
| 1 | +4 |
| 2 | −6 |
| 3 | −2 |
| 4 | +8 |
| 5 | −3 |
| 6 | +5 |
| 7 | −7 |
| 8 | +1 |

**Step 1 — Mean bias (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i = \frac{(+4) + (-6) + (-2) + (+8) + (-3) + (+5) + (-7) + (+1)}{8}
$$

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{0}{8} = 0 \text{ ms}
$$

(The positive and negative differences exactly cancel out.)

**Step 2 — Standard deviation of differences (2 marks):**

$$
s_d = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - \bar{d})^2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{7} \sum_{i=1}^{8} d_i^2}
$$

| $i$ | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | +4 | 16 |
| 2 | −6 | 36 |
| 3 | −2 | 4 |
| 4 | +8 | 64 |
| 5 | −3 | 9 |
| 6 | +5 | 25 |
| 7 | −7 | 49 |
| 8 | +1 | 1 |

**Sum of squares:**

$$
\sum d_i^2 = 16 + 36 + 4 + 64 + 9 + 25 + 49 + 1 = 204
$$

**Standard deviation:**

$$
s_d = \sqrt{\frac{204}{7}} = \sqrt{29.143} \approx 5.40 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — 95 % Limits of Agreement (2 marks):**

$$
\text{LLA} = \bar{d} - 1.96 \, s_d = 0 - 1.96 \times 5.40 = 0 - 10.58 = -10.58 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\text{ULA} = \bar{d} + 1.96 \, s_d = 0 + 1.96 \times 5.40 = 0 + 10.58 = +10.58 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance check (1 mark):**

The acceptance band is **± 15 ms**. Since LLA = −10.58 ms and ULA = +10.58 ms both lie **inside ± 15 ms**, the wearable ECG is **clinically interchangeable** with the reference monitor for R-R interval measurement.

**Final conclusion (to be quoted in the report):**

> *"The wearable ECG demonstrated zero mean bias ($\bar{d} = 0$ ms) and 95 % limits of agreement of −10.58 to +10.58 ms against the Philips Intellivue MX450. Since the limits are within the clinically acceptable ± 15 ms band, the device is equivalent to the reference for R-R interval measurement."*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "correlation" with "agreement"** (loses 1–2 marks in any Bland-Altman question). Always state: *correlation ≠ agreement*.
> 2. **Forgetting the "− 1" in the denominator** of the standard-deviation formula. Use $N-1$, not $N$, for the sample standard deviation of differences.
> 3. **Skipping the "acceptance band" comparison.** Computing LLA/ULA without stating whether they fall inside the clinically acceptable range is a half-answer. Always close with a *clinical equivalence statement*.
> 4. **Omitting the FMEA "Detectability" dimension.** Many students compute RPN using only $S \times O$, ignoring $D$. The examiner deducts 1 mark immediately.
> 5. **Citing the wrong standard.** A pulse-oximeter project must cite **ISO 80601-2-61**, a defibrillator must cite **IEC 60601-2-4**, an infusion pump must cite **IEC 60601-2-24**. Citing the generic **IEC 60601-1** alone is acceptable but weak; the *particular standard* scores higher.
> 6. **No budget justification.** A BOM table without a per-component justification (datasheet reference) loses 1 mark in the procurement section.
> 7. **No ethics statement.** Any project involving human volunteers must include an **Informed Consent Form** and (in India) **ICMR National Ethical Guidelines 2017** compliance. Missing this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 8. **Single-person credit.** If the project diary does not show **task allocation across all team members**, the external examiner may withhold the "teamwork" marks entirely.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it above your desk before the exam.**

- [ ] **Five phases** of the project: **Topic Selection → Literature Review → Analysis → Design/Dev → Testing/Validation**. Memorize the order and the gate reviews.
- [ ] **PICO framework** = Population + Intervention + Comparator + Outcome. Use it to convert a vague idea into a testable hypothesis.
- [ ] **Feasibility matrix** typically uses 5 criteria: Clinical Relevance (0.25), Technical Feasibility (0.30), Innovation (0.15), Budget (0.20), Regulatory Awareness (0.10). Acceptance threshold: weighted score **≥ 7.0**.
- [ ] **Literature review must end with a "Research Gap Statement"** — a 1–2 sentence claim of *what is missing in prior work that your project will fill*. This is non-negotiable.
- [ ] **Standards to memorize:**
  - **IEC 60601-1** — General medical electrical equipment safety.
  - **ISO 13485** — Quality management system for medical devices.
  - **ISO 14971** — Risk management for medical devices (FMEA lives here).
  - **ISO 80601-2-61** — Particular standard for pulse oximeters.
  - **IEC 60601-2-25** — Particular standard for ECG.
  - **IEC 60601-2-30** — Particular standard for NIBP (automated sphygmomanometers).
  - **IEC 60601-2-49** — Particular standard for patient monitors.
- [ ] **Bland-Altman formulas:** $\bar{d}$, $s_d = \sqrt{\sum(d_i - \bar{d})^2 / (N-1)}$, LLA = $\bar{d} - 1.96 s_d$, ULA = $\bar{d} + 1.96 s_d$. Always compare with a **clinical acceptance band**.
- [ ] **FMEA formula:** RPN = S × O × D. Threshold for mandatory mitigation = **100**. Re-score after each design control to show risk reduction.
- [ ] **BOM target utilization** = 50–70 % of the budget. < 50 % is wasteful; > 90 % is risky.
- [ ] **Critical path** in a 16-week semester must be ≤ 16 weeks. If exceeded, parallelize and document the risk mitigation plan.
- [ ] **Volunteer validation** requires: (i) **Informed Consent Form**, (ii) **ICMR 2017 ethical guidelines** compliance, (iii) **N ≥ 5** for a pilot study, **N ≥ 30** for a publishable clinical study.
- [ ] **Teamwork evidence** in the report: include a **RACI matrix**, a **Gantt chart**, and a **project diary** signed weekly by the guide.
- [ ] **Final report structure:** Title page → Certificate → Acknowledgement → Abstract → Contents → Introduction → Literature Review → Methodology → Design & Development → Testing & Validation → Results & Discussion → Conclusion → Future Scope → References (IEEE numbered) → Appendices (datasheets, schematics, code listing, consent form).
- [ ] **Presentation rule of thumb:** 10–12 slides, 10–12 minutes, 1 slide per minute. Always end with a **live demo of the prototype** (or a 30-second video backup if hardware fails).
- [ ] **Examiner's hidden question:** *"If your device fails on the demo day, what would you do?"* — Always have a **mitigation slide** showing: (a) backup video, (b) pre-recorded serial-monitor logs, (c) simulation result in MATLAB/LTspice as a Plan B.
